channel.title: eFinancialCareers Editorial Feed
channel.description: Browse news and career advice from the financial services, investment banking, fintech industry. Get expert insight into what it's like to work in finance.

item.title: Citi has a new women's advocate: it's that hard-driving Andy Sieg
item.description:
Andy Sieg, </news/andy-sieg-investigation> the head of wealth at Citi, is an interesting figure. Feted for getting things done and for driving change in Citi's wealth business, Sieg is popular with CEO Jane Fraser </news/andy-sieg-wealth-citi> who personally visited his home to hire him. However, Sieg has also been the subject of complaints </news/andy-sieg-citi-sarcasm> by senior Citi women who claim he's subjected them to "expletive filled rants" and shared secret songs </news/citi-andy-sieg-case>. Both Citi and Sieg have steadfastly denied any wrongdoing. This is fortunate, because Sieg has now been chairing women's events for the bank.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Financial News <https://www.fnlondon.com/amp/articles/citis-andy-sieg-hosts-international-womens-day-event-after-hr-probe-f91a6099>reported today that Sieg hosted a fireside chat with tennis player Chris Evert for a Citi women's history event. Posting about the chat on his LinkedIn page, Sieg said Evert's conversation taught him that: “What champions do, when their backs are to the wall, they find a way to win. They know they’ve got to change, take risks, play outside the box."
At least one female colleague at Citi agrees. Natalya Alexei, an MD in Citi's private bank who was hired by Sieg from Silicon Valley Bank in October 2025, said she was honoured to attend the talk between Sieg and Evert, which made her reflect upon her own career journey. Many of the other people reacting favourably to Sieg's post were men.
Sieg's conversation has not gone unnoticed on Wall Street Discriminates <https://wallstreetdiscriminates.com/>, the site where women anonymously share stories </news/some-female-citi-m-ds-created-a-site-to-share-women-s-banking-horror-stories> about perceived wrongdoing on Wall Street. Various posts purporting to be from women at the bank have observed the "irony" of Sieg running a women's day event following complaints against him and the departure of senior women like Ida Liu, the global head of private banking who is now at HSBC. One Citi insider suggested that Sieg's comment above referred to his own back being against the wall but winning through despite complaints.
Citi declined to comment. The bank said previously that it thoroughly investigated all the allegations against Sieg. That investigation was undertaken by law firm Paul Weiss whose chairman recently resigned. </news/brad-karp-epstein>The Financial Times <https://www.ft.com/content/be4ab80a-40f8-4cb2-81a3-cde101b5fa7a>claimed in October that Paul Weiss completed the investigation without interviewing some of the women involved.
Sieg's was not Citi's only women's initiative. Posting on Instagram, <https://www.instagram.com/p/DVYoMj4DgsQ/>the bank said it "comes together" every March to celebrate female agility and leadership.
Sieg's wife, Heliane Steden, is a tennis player and Sieg may therefore know Evert personally as a result. Their fireside chat was co-hosted by Kate Luft, </news/citi-reorg-mike-mason> Citi's recently promoted head of retail banking who is the sister of Citi head of HR, Sarah Wechter.
Have a confidential story, tip, or comment you’d like to share? Contact: +44 7537 182250 (SMS, Whatsapp or voicemail). Telegram: @SarahButcher. Signal: sarahbutcher.22 Click here to fill in our anonymous form </about/editor-tips>, or email editortips@efinancialcareers.com.

item.title: UBS wants its US junior bankers in the office 5 days a week now
item.description:
If you're a junior banker at UBS you might be working yourself into a state of insurgency. Bonuses at the Swiss bank appear to have been acceptable </news/ubs-markets-bonuses>, but people purporting to be juniors at the bank are forever complaining about their lives on Wall Street Oasis <https://www.wallstreetoasis.com/>.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Their complaints include the alleged removal of perks. One such perk was the option to work from home on Fridays. While junior bankers at JPMorgan </news/jpmorgan-return-to-office-five-days> and Goldman Sachs have been in the office full time for over a year, junior bankers at UBS have benefitted from a day in their homes. Not any more. The Americas banking business wants them in the office five days a week.
UBS declined to comment on the new office protocol, but it's understood to be real. It only applies in the US. Junior bankers at UBS in London can still spend Fridays at home. Staff in some central functions at the Swiss bank can spend Mondays there too.
Not all the whinging on Wall Street Oasis is valid. Earlier this month, there were complaints on the site that UBS was cutting its meal allowances </news/meal-allowances> for junior bankers. We understand that this is not so. More recently, there have been claims that junior bankers at UBS in America are unionising. We understand that this is not so either.
UBS is not alone in liking to see people face to face. Earlier this week, HSBC also asked its Hong Kong client-facing staff to appear in person <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-10/hsbc-scraps-work-from-home-for-client-facing-staff-in-hong-kong>every day.
82250 (SMS, Whatsapp or voicemail). Telegram: @SarahButcher. Signal: sarahbutcher.22 Click here to fill in our anonymous form </about/editor-tips>, or email editortips@efinancialcareers.com.

post covid a hybrid model depending on the business and roles - traders were always five days in the office
some gorup functions 3 day minimum
global banking business in Europe is still four days in the office and one day from home

item.title: London banking recruiters deluged with irrelevant CVs from the Middle East
item.description:
If you're based in the UAE and have decided that you might like a job in London or Singapore, then good luck. Some headhunters say they are now overwhelmed with applications from people trying to flee the region.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
"We are being flooded with irrelevant CVs from the Middle East," says one London front office </news/front-office-middle-office-back-office-banking> headhunter, speaking on condition of anonymity. "Everyone is desperate to get out. If they're cut, they know they won't be able to find new jobs within 90 days."
It's relatively easy for employers to terminate people in the Middle East. The DIFC specifies <https://www.lexology.com/library/detail.aspx?g=1a76f0a3-17a5-486b-bcd7-11a5ec2bec03> that contracts must have a notice period of 30 days, but expats in Dubai typically have a visa-related grace period lasting only 90 days in which to look for a new job after they're terminated. If they can't find one, they have to leave. If they don't leave, they are fined for "immigration violations."
In the circumstances, Europe suddenly looks like a good option.
Investment banking recruiters in London have mixed reports on the health of the local hiring market in the wake of the war. Logan Naidu, founder and CEO of Dartmouth Partners, says nothing's changed: "There has been no hesitation or hiring slowdown so far. Clients either have not seen a slowdown in revenues or expect this to deescalate." Another M&A headhunter says search mandates are still being awarded but it's quiet: "Everyone was super positive in early January but that's faded fast. There's really not much hiring going on now," he adds. This is despite claims <https://www.fnlondon.com/articles/wall-street-execs-point-to-investment-banking-fee-jump-as-iran-war-complicates-dealmaking-e4e841af> by Citi and Bank of America this week that their investment banking revenues will rise by double digit percentages year-on-year in the first quarter.
Markets jobs that benefit from volatility should hold up better than the banking jobs reliant on a stable deal environment. However, hiring processes here are being slowed by the chaos of the ever-changing situation. Russell Clarke, a partner at fixed income search firm Mantis Partners in London, says both clients and candidates are busy amidst "heightened volatility" and are "prioritizing deal flow across financing and investment activities." Recruitment is not the priority.
This is bad news if you're trying to line up a new job outside the UAE. It is even worse news if you're applying wildly for vacancies that probably don't suit your profile anyway.

item.title: Did Revolut need people from investment banks to surmount its biggest challenge?
item.description:
Nik Storonsky </news/why-working-for-revolut-founder-nik-storonsky-is-a-pleasure>has never been shy about his preference for hiring young upstarts over experienced corporate types </news/the-most-exciting-and-bizarre-job-for-24-year-olds-at-revolut-and-other-fintechs#:~:text=Storonsky%20said%20he%20hired%20%22experienced%20executives%22%20(said%20with%20a%20sarcastic%20twang)%2C%20who%20%22failed%20miserably.%22> with big names on their CV. </news/revolut-career-harvester-cv> While that has paid dividends in making Revolut the most recognizable fintech in Europe, it still faced criticism over its inability to land a UK banking license. Now, at last, it has achieved one, <https://www.ft.com/content/b4df4126-351e-4424-9707-8a12ca6b79a6> and it seemingly had help getting there from some corporate veterans.
Follow eFinancialCareers on Google News here. <https://news.google.com/publications/CAAqMggKIixDQklTR3dnTWFoY0tGV1ZtYVc1aGJtTnBZV3hqWVhKbFpYSnpMbU52YlNnQVAB?ceid=GB:en&oc=3>
Over the past year, Revolut hired at least 22 regulatory staff in the UK, the majority of which came from either banks or consultancies. The most senior hire was Edinburgh-based Mark Brydson, who joined in August to work on statistical reporting oversight; he spent the last two decades at Morgan Stanley, where he was an executive director and EMEA financial reporting assurance lead. He arrived just a few months before regulators delayed the approval of Revolut's UK license over the bank's risk controls.
Since then, progress has evidently been made. Coincidentally or not, Brydson brought in some ex-colleagues from Morgan Stanley during that time. In November it hired Michael Paton, who spent seven years at Morgan Stanley but was most recently a regulatory reporting VP at Barclays. Chris Reilly also joined in November from Morgan Stanley's ESG quality assurance team; Brydson also held the role of global ESG internal assurance lead at Morgan Stanley. David O'Brien joined from Bank of America's global liquidity management team in January, but he too spent five years at Morgan Stanley. All three men are based in Scotland alongside Brydson.
It's not just Scotsmen from Morgan Stanley joining Revolut's regulatory team. Recent hires include two ex-JPMorgan employees, two ex-HSBC and seven with experience at a Big Four firm. </news/working-for-the-big-four-pwc-deloitte-ey-kpmg>
Perhaps working in Revolut's compliance team gives something that a bank or consultancy doesn't. Insiders at investment banks have frequently complained to us that their work is unfulfilling </news/compliance-jobs-finance-trap>and under-resourced. </news/how-compliance-became-the-worst-place-to-work-in-banking> Solving one of Revolut's most historic bugbears may have been a more intellectually stimulating challenge.
Have a confidential story, tip, or comment you’d like to share? Bear with us if you leave a comment at the bottom of this article: comments are moderated intermittently by human beings. Sometimes these humans might be asleep, or away from their desks, so it may take a while for your comment to appear. You must take sole responsibility for comments you post on this site. item.title: Deutsche Bank MD bonuses are up 60% in two years, but there's a catch
item.description:
Deutsche Bank's annual report for 2025 is out, and with it, the bank's compensation report. There is good and bad news within it.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
The good news is that bonuses are up for everyone. They are especially higher for managing directors (MDs) and others categorised as 'material risk takers' (MRTs).
The bad news is that Deutsche Bank is still deferring its compensation heavily, and will continue to do so for the foreseeable future. As a European bank, it remains governed by punitive European compensation rules.
Deutsche Bank's overall bonus pool for last year was €2.7bn, an 8% increase on 2024. In the investment bank specifically, the bonus pool was €1.3bn, and within the investment bank a bonus pool of €644m went to a small pool of material risk takers.
The outcomes for pay per head at Deutsche's investment bank are shown in the charts below. The average of Deutsche Bank's 8,188 employees earned a total of €330k for last year in combined salary and bonus. The average of Deutsche's 634 MRTs in the investment bank earned €1.8m. One unnamed individual at Deutsche Bank earned between €17m and €18m.
As the chart below shows, average bonuses for Deutsche Bank's investment bank MRTs have risen by 57% in two years. In 2023, the bank was paying its investment bank MRTs average bonuses of €646k. Last year, it paid €1m.
Deutsche Bank's senior people might be happy about their bonus increases, were it not for the fact that their bonuses are heavily deferred. Deutsche said today that MRTs in its 'Material Business Units' are subject to 50% deferrals and that if anyone there earns a salary of €600k+, the entirety of their bonus will be deferred. The average MRT salary in the investment bank is €795k.
Deutsche's keenness for withholding bonuses comes as non-EU banks in London are benefitting from a relaxation of the EU bonus rules. Liberated banks are free to pay bonuses that are many multiples of salary </news/bonus-cap> while Deutsche Bank is stuck paying bonuses that are twice salary or less.
Non-EU banks can also pay a higher proportion of their bonuses in cash. The UK government ruled last year </news/banking-bonuses-london> that MRTs can be defined more loosely and that 60% of their bonuses up to £660k can be cash and 40% of bonuses beyond that. Deutsche Bank doesn't operate under UK rules; it looks like most of its MRTS have 100% of their bonuses deferred.
Have a confidential story, tip, or comment you’d like to share? Contact: +44 7537 182250 (SMS, Whatsapp or voicemail). Telegram: @SarahButcher. Signal: sarahbutcher.22 Click here to fill in our anonymous form </about/editor-tips>, or email editortips@efinancialcareers.com.

item.title: Morning Coffee: Goldman Sachs’ golden partner said the wrong thing on a client call. If you want a banking job now, try Hong Kong
item.description:
One of the things that superstar traders often find difficult to cope with when they move into senior management roles is to moderate their language. It’s not just a matter of remembering to use swear words judiciously; the whole culture of a high pressure trading desk tends toward braggadocio, “banter” and dark humour. The sort of remark which comes over as sharp and witty on a squawk box can appear to be really quite tone deaf to a more formal audience.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Kunal Shah <https://www.ft.com/content/9232dbce-0255-4949-8c4c-ea58d86a4166>, the co-head of Goldman Sachs’ fixed income currencies and commodities (FICC) trading business is presumably more than aware than this today. On a client call scheduled to discuss the current war in Iran, he remarked that some Goldman clients were “just glad that there’s something to talk about that isn’t software exposures and private credit … this is at least a distraction from that”.
Ouch. Goldman Sachs’ press office have responded that Shah “was asked a question about what he was hearing from clients and shared his observations from multiple points of view”, but this seems to miss the point. Nobody in private credit really thinks that burning ships and drone bombs have a silver lining, and nobody really thinks that the geopolitical chaos is any kind of media win that will stop anyone from noticing problems with their marks to market <https://www.ft.com/content/389a0003-d8de-4afd-9de9-be6e9fc6888c>. If any client said that to Shah, in so many words, then it’s much more likely that they too were just making an off-colour joke, and probably would have been surprised to hear it repeated out of context.
The gaffe is unlikely to damage Shah’s personal franchise or popularity, though. The hedge fund clients who were the main target of the call will, for the most part, have grown up in the same culture. To complain about a tasteless remark would be the ultimate loss of face, showing an unforgivable failure of sang-froid which might cast doubt on one’s ability to take risks. Even if they had been in the process of evacuating their office and stockpiling food <https://www.thebanker.com/content/1d65b2a1-c90e-4d1d-a596-19488eb0ff40> while listening to the call.
It’s just a little bit embarrassing; presumably Kunal Shah will be cringing a bit while listening to the playback. And it’s a reminder that his own rise through the ranks </news/2019/06/goldman-sachs-resilience> (MD at 27, Partner at 31 and co-head of FICC at the age of 41) has been so meteoric that he hasn’t quite had enough time for all the rough edges to be knocked off. We should probably treasure remarks like this, though, as they show that for the time being, there are still some top bankers who haven’t been rendered completely bland by media training.
Elsewhere, the Hong Kong IPO market has got so hot that some banks are no longer able to cover all the bases with their own staff. Morgan Stanley <https://www.reuters.com/business/finance/morgan-stanley-hires-contract-staff-hong-kong-deals-surge-sources-say-2026-03-11/>, for example, has put together a “transaction support team” to help carry out due diligence on new issues, and have had to go into the short term contractor market and hire people on temporary one-year contracts.
This is not unknown in the industry – due diligence is something of a commodity skill, which can also be bought in from accountancy and consulting firms. But it’s not at all common in the Asian market, and it’s a sign that the pressure must have reached levels at which it’s become literally impossible to execute the pipeline even if you abandon all the HR protections on junior bankers.
There are plenty of other signs of stress across the whole region. HSBC is cancelling remote working <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-10/hsbc-scraps-work-from-home-for-client-facing-staff-in-hong-kong?embedded-checkout=true> for front office </news/front-office-middle-office-back-office-banking> staff, while Standard Chartered <https://www.ft.com/content/716253b0-762b-4578-84f2-01354e71b2c4> has announced plans to double the number of bankers at its high-net worth unit in Singapore. As the rest of the world wakes up every morning wondering what the next shock is, it seems like China and its financial centres are very firmly back in business.
Meanwhile …
Threats from Iran to strike banks in Dubai are being taken seriously. Employees at Goldman Sachs, Citi and other firms are being told to work from home, and although the DIFC has not been evacuated, people are being told to seek permission before going into offices across the Middle East. (Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-11/goldman-citi-tell-staff-to-exit-dubai-offices-amid-iran-threats>)
The analysts at Cairn Capital use some unusual metrics. They spotted that Paresh Raja, the finance director of Market Financial Solutions, seemed to be wearing a £200,000 watch while living in a £400,000 house, and this application of the “watch/house ratio” kept them out of one of the biggest recent private credit blow-ups. (FT <https://www.ft.com/content/1c1144d1-bad1-4575-a00e-0431dbab0c8f>)
Jefferies, on the other hand, did not do the watch/house check, and were among the lenders to MFS, and quite a few other “high profile situations”. Rich Handler is now arguing publicly with the CEO of Western Alliance Bancorp, which is suing Jefferies over the extent to which “non-recourse” loan facilities meant what they said. (Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-11/jefferies-faces-legal-pressure-on-first-brands-alleged-fraud-exposure?srnd=homepage-uk&embedded-checkout=true>)
The Pentagon is staffing up its new private equity team. It wants to hire 30 bankers, to invest $200bn in the defence (or War, presumably) sector. As well as being an opportunity to accelerate the sale of stock compensation, and to play with “more capital than most investors deploy in their entire careers”, it won’t have escaped bankers’ notice that the opportunity is “not a career move, but a two-to-three year secondment”, so you won’t even have to stick around to find out whether the deals worked out or not. (Semafor <https://www.semafor.com/article/03/11/2026/pentagon-headhunting-goldman-jpmorgan-bankers-for-economic-defense-unit>)
After many years, Revolut has finally got a full UK banking licence. (Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-11/revolut-set-to-secure-full-uk-banking-license-unlocking-lending?srnd=homepage-uk>)
The latest flex for super-rich and important people seems to be to write emails with absolutely terrible spelling and grammar, leaving it to lesser mortals to work out what they mean. If your boss tries this affectation, it is officially acceptable to ask him if he’s pretending to be Jeffrey Epstein. (WSJ <https://d.docs.live.net/30567645f2749223/Documents/morning%20coffee/220626.docx>)
It is almost disappointing to find out that, in a week of such high drama for natural gas markets, Pierre Andurand’s “Commodities Discretionary Enhanced Fund”, famous for its wild swings in either direction, was 6% up last week. (Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-11/oil-trader-pierre-andurand-made-6-last-week-in-chaotic-market>)
Meanwhile, Caxton Global Macro is down 7% … (FT <https://www.ft.com/content/f47e1478-7b4b-4dd3-8037-6643549ac737>)
… and the big multistrats are mostly nursing mid-single digit losses, with DE Shaw as the stand-out exception. (Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-11/de-shaw-s-hedge-funds-gain-in-a-brutal-week-for-multistrat-peers?taid=69b17cb2bee12000015e0fa9&utm_campaign=trueanthem&utm_content=business&utm_medium=social&utm_source=twitter26-03-11/de-shaw-s-hedge-funds-gain-in-a-brutal-week-for-multistrat-peers?taid=69b17cb2bee12000015e0fa9&utm_campaign=trueanthem&utm_content=business&utm_medium=social&utm_source=twitter>)

item.title: Bank of America's European high yield trading team sprung a leak
item.description:
Bank of America's bonuses are paid and people there are moving on. The London high yield trading team has experienced several hasty exits.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Bank of America isn't commenting, but we understand that two people have left and that more may follow. Robert Laux, the head of European high yield trading at BofA, is understood to have quit and to be enroute to UBS. Justin Mullem, a more junior credit trading, is said to be off to Barclays, and other members of the team are allegedly open to moving on too.
It's not clear what the issue is - or indeed whether there is one. Bank of America's fixed income bonuses for 2025 are thought to have been "not bad." </news/bank-of-america-bonuses-are-out-they-may-be-moderately-good>
Robert Laux leaves his brother behind at BofA. Nick Laux runs global equities at the US bank, after joining from Morgan Stanley in 2023. There was some early whinging about EMEA equities bonuses at BofA </news/at-bank-of-america-some-equities-traders-would-like-bigger-bonuses>, so Nick could maybe call his brother once he's settled in.
Bank of America strengthened its high yield credit trading team last year when it recruited Gajern Kailavasan from Deutsche Bank. Kailavasan is a rising star. However, he worked with Laux and may now be exhibiting less shine.

item.title: Yes, Citadel Securities hired the Goldman Sachs partner that vanished
item.description:
When Nikhil Choraria </news/nikhil-choraria> disappeared from Goldman Sachs last month, the rumour was that he was joining Citadel Securities. We were given to understand that this was not so, but now we understand that it is. Surprise!
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Choraria is joining many former Goldman friends and colleagues at the electronic market firm, where we understand that he will be co-head of fixed income alongside Shyam Rajan, the global head of fixed income who once worked for Bank of America.
Choraria will be based in London and will report directly to Citadel Securities CEO Peng Zhao.
He will find many familiar faces about the place. Citadel Securities also employs Nohshad Shah, who was Goldman's former co-head of rates sales. It employs Frank Flight </news/citadel-securities-is-making-its-ex-goldman-sachs-macro-vp-famous>, a fresh faced strategist who writes densely worded opinions on the market. Further afield is Jim Esposito </news/jim-esposito-citadel-securities-president>, the cheerful ex-wrestler who is now Citadel Securities' president after decades at Goldman Sachs.
Choraria was said to have been one of Goldman's top earning traders </news/citi-s-top-inflation-trader-is-joining-brevan-howard-goldman-s-is-a-man-of-mystery>. Goldman Sachs is likely to miss him. Hopefully Goldman will not engage in tactics similar to JPMorgan, </news/jpmorgan-citadel-securities> which is curtailing some of its interactions with Citadel Securities after its head of high touch equity sales trading was poached.
Citadel Securities has made various hires for its rates team. In February, it hired Andrew Buonauro </news/citadel-securities-is-growing-its-rates-business-with-a-squarepoint-capital-trading-head> from Squarepoint. Last March, Citadel Securities hired Keith Cynar from Goldman to run rates sales in the US.
It's not clear how Rajan feels about his new co-head. Rajan joined Citadel Securities in 2022 after a year at hedge fund LMR Partners and three years as an MD in rates trading at Bank of America. Choraria is the more experienced of the two.

item.title: "Non-French bankers are leaving Paris because of the French. It has nothing to do with tax"
item.description:
I read an article </news/bankers-in-paris-are-ready-to-return-to-london-as-tax-deals-run-out>on this site yesterday about bankers leaving Paris as their tax deals expire. There is more to it than that.
I spent six years in Paris and thankfully I am now out. Leaving has nothing to with tax. It has everything to do with the Parisians.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
If you go to Paris to work in a bank and you think it will be like London, you will have a shock. Paris is as parochial as Frankfurt but worse. People in Paris are also rude.
Paris is not an inclusive place. The Parisians do not want people from overseas among them. It's beautiful there but the French are like a mafia. Within banks they cluster together, collude and edge overseas people out. I'm not the only one who's experienced this - friends have had the same experience.
This is why so many foreigners have left. If you look at overseas banks in Paris now you will see that almost everyone there is French. This is what the French bankers wanted: it has been a full French takeover.
It's also why there are so few hedge fund there. Funds are meritocratic and hire from all over the world. Until now, they've preferred to hire people into Dubai and Abu Dhabi, which are much more welcoming to non-nationals. Even after the past week, many people in the UAE would never choose Paris. It's best avoided, in my opinion.

item.title: HSBC's Hong Kong equity researchers feel forsaken after London promotions
item.description:
As we reported earlier this month </news/hsbc-s-md-promotions-are-back-to-normal-but-never-simple>, HSBC has promoted some managing directors (MDs). Very few of those managing directors are in research.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
HSBC's list of newly rendered research MDs is visible here <https://www.research.hsbc.com/R/20/DtXRHLc?sharing_user=f81e686d-db8c-4ffc-a53a-7893a8a8bd4b&network_shared_to=linkedin&utm_medium=organic&utm_source=everyonesocial&es_id=5f2419729e>. There are two: Mike Tyndall and Rajesh Kumar. Both are in London.
Tyndall is HSBC's senior global autos analyst. He joined the bank from Citi in 2018 and was a director for seven years until his promotion. Henning Cosman, HSBC's former head of automotive research, left in 2022. He is now at Barclays.
Kumar is HSBC's senior global healthcare analyst, based in London. He spent nearly two decades at the bank before ascending to the highest rank.
HSBC trimmed its equities team </news/hsbc-s-pre-bonus-equities-job-cuts> before bonuses, cutting James Grafton, its global head of cash equities trading and Steve Jobber, its head of cash equities trading for EMEA, the day before bonuses were announced.
Last year, HSBC also cut swathes of its equity traders </news/hsbc-s-latest-cuts-the-senior-people-stayed>in EMEA after also jettisoning its investment bank </news/hscb-closure-europe-us-job-cuts>(excluding debt capital markets) in Europe and the US. In the circumstances, HSBC's Asian equity researchers were expected to be favoured in MD promotions. This didn't happen, although Achal Kumar, Aris Dacanay, both of whom are Asia-based, were promoted at director level, so maybe it's all fine.
HSBC didn't respond to a request to comment.

item.title: Deutsche Bank hired a Morgan Stanley MD who claimed to be falsely accused of upsetting colleagues
item.description:
Last year, Morgan Stanley's </news/morgan-stanley-goldman-sachs-job-cuts-2026> global head of commodities, </news/2023/02/commodities-trading-jobs> Kow Attah-Mensah, departed under mysterious circumstances. Despite having unfinished business with his former employer, he has managed to find a new opportunity at Deutsche bank. </news/deutsche-bank-mds-2026>
Follow eFinancialCareers on Google News here. <https://news.google.com/publications/CAAqMggKIixDQklTR3dnTWFoY0tGV1ZtYVc1aGJtTnBZV3hqWVhKbFpYSnpMbU52YlNnQVAB?ceid=GB:en&oc=3>
Attah-Mensah is joining Deutsche Bank in New York as its global head of commodities. Although the bank wound down parts of its commodities team back in 2013, </news/2013/12/where-the-poor-laid-off-deutsche-bank-commodities-traders-are-going-to-get-rehired>it kept around a smaller, focused group to invest in commodities derivatives, which Attah-Mensah now takes charge of.
It's a comeback for Attah-Mensah who has been out of the market since last March. He spent 18 years at Morgan Stanley, making MD in 2017 <https://www.morganstanley.com/press-releases/morgan-stanley-appoints-140-new-managing-directors>, and held various senior roles in its commodities business. He served as co-head of power and gas structuring </news/2015/02/really-like-work-structurer-investment-bank>, then as head of North American power and gas before being promoted to head commodities in its entirety.
Attah-Mensah was allegedly terminated by Morgan Stanley after an internal investigation regarding interactions with his colleagues. His lawyer, John D. Singer, told Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2025-04-25/morgan-stanley-fired-its-commodities-chief-after-misconduct-probe>that this investigation was "bogus" and that Attah-Mensah is "an employee of the utmost veracity, loyalty and honor." Attah-Mensah plans to prosecute his former employer; his lawyer has a strong track record in similar cases </news/kow-atta-mensah-john-singer>which suggests he might be successful.
A Deutsche Bank insider said that the bank conducted a thorough review of Attah-Mensah's background prior to his arrival and is confident he will uphold the bank's own conduct standards. Deutsche Bank declined to comment.

item.title: Hedge fund Rokos hired a macro trader from Citadel in London
item.description:
With all the current geopolitical issues, </news/hedge-fund-job-fears-after-tempestuous-week> it's a turbulent time to be a macro trader </news/london-fixed-income-macro-pay>. Paul Mrockowski, who spent the past three and a half years at Citadel, has the added challenge of adapting to a big new role at a new employer.
Follow eFinancialCareers on Google News here. <https://news.google.com/publications/CAAqMggKIixDQklTR3dnTWFoY0tGV1ZtYVc1aGJtTnBZV3hqWVhKbFpYSnpMbU52YlNnQVAB?ceid=GB:en&oc=3>
Mrockowski is joining Rokos Capital Management, the hedge fund of ex-Goldman Sachs partner Chris Rokos, as its head of portfolio trading </news/2017/08/portfolio-trading-jobs>. This is the practice of selling large baskets of securities; it has historically been popular in credit but has also taken off </news/quantitative-fixed-income-traders#:~:text=As%20fixed%20income%20portfolio%20trading%20takes%20off%20and%20old%20school%20credit%20traders%20disappear%2C%20fixed%20income%20traders%20who%20can%20operate%20in%20electronic%20markets%20are%20poised%20to%20become%20the%20most%20sought%20after%20people%20of%202025.%20And%20not%20just%20by%20banks.%C2%A0> across other areas of fixed income in recent years thanks to the electronification of markets.
Mrockowski was also previously a portfolio manager at Saudi-linked conglomerate Olayan Group. There, he traded sovereign credit, rates and foreign exchange. He also worked as an MD at Singapore </news/singapore-bankers-work-harder-than-hong-kong-colleagues-for-less> macro hedge fund Graticule Asset Management.
Rokos was one of the many hedge funds to lose money in the chaotic start to March. Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-10/balyasny-millennium-point72-hit-by-iran-conflict-in-wild-week> said it lost 0.17% last week, bringing its year-to-date returns down to 2.3%. It's a modest drop compared to some rivals; Bloomberg said ExodusPoint had lost its entire year-to-date gains after being up 2.6% in the first two months of the year.

item.title: Mizuho's head of credit trading had a long absence and then suddenly disappeared
item.description:
If you're looking for Aristo Vounakis at Mizuho in London, you will not find him there. He is not there. He has gone. But he wasn't around for a while anyway.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Sources say that Vounakis was let go by Mizuho this week. Mizuho confirmed his exit but declined to elaborate upon the circumstances. Vounakis himself didn't respond to a request to comment on his disappearance.
Vounakis was Mizuho's head of credit trading in Europe. He joined the Japanese bank as head of corporate credit trading in 2019 after time at RBS and Lloyds, and was promoted to head of flow credit trading in 2022.
Multiple sources say that Vounakis was off sick for eight months before he returned to the desk and was asked to leave.
Go on sick leave and then disappearing is not unheard of on Mizuho's credit desk. Claus Jorgensen </news/2022/04/claus-jorgensen-hsbc-mizuho>, a former head of credit trading at the bank, did the same four years ago.
It's not clear what Vounakis plans to do next. It's thought that he has a nice house in Crete but lives in London. It's also thought that he was one of the biggest producers on the Mizuho credit desk, alongside Hardeep Bariana who was promoted as his co-head in 2024. Bariana remains at the bank.
Vounakis is the latest exit from Mizuho's credit desk. Other departures include Davide Ceruti and Bader Barghoti, who've gone to SMBC.

item.title: Morning Coffee: A new path to massive paydays for London bankers. Goldman Sachs starts to invent the new Big Short
item.description:
If there’s one message that we’ve consistently tried to get across at Morning Coffee, it’s that it’s extremely rare for anyone to walk out of an employment court feeling like a winner. No matter how rock solid your case it, it’s almost always best to try to work things out amicably and carry on with your career rather than lawyering up. But the rare exceptions to that rule might be getting somewhat less rare, in London at least, thanks to some new employment legislation <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/features/2026-03-10/new-employment-rights-act-gives-bankers-opportunity-to-win-huge-payouts?taid=69afa763f9dd4700013ed36d&utm_campaign=trueanthem&utm_content=business&utm_medium=social&utm_source=twitter&embedded-checkout=true> coming into force at the start of next year.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Historically, London bankers have faced the problem that employment tribunal awards for unfair dismissal </news/unlimited-compensation-for-unfair-dismissal-bankers-uk> are capped at the relatively low sum of £118,223 (US$158k). This means that for anyone in a front office </news/front-office-middle-office-back-office-banking>role, and for all but the most junior ranks in mid-office, it’s simply not worth bothering; the amount you could possibly win is likely to be less than half the basic salary you miss out on during the litigation, let alone the bonus. (It’s not like the USA, where it’s possible to win a seven-figure settlement </news/centerview-analyst-sleep-working-hours> after only three months on the analyst program).
The exception to this rule has always been that cases are not capped in cases of discrimination. This has led to a number of protracted legal battles over what constitutes ageism </news/2022/07/ageism-banking> and the fine line between “banter </news/fined-nicknames-bnp-paribas>” and a hostile workplace environment. In a number of cases </news/unlimited-compensation-for-unfair-dismissal-bankers-uk>, bankers who didn’t feel that they had a colourable discrimination case have demanded to be given their jobs back </news/unlimited-compensation-for-unfair-dismissal-bankers-uk>, either claiming that they could still work with their old colleagues, or angling to get a more favourable out of court deal.
But now, these sorts of shenanigans will no longer be necessary. The latest UK Employment Rights Act has, among many other measures, lifted the cap on claims in cases of simple unfair dismissal. This might end up embarrassing the government somewhat, if the earliest beneficiaries of a piece of legislation which was meant to benefit ordinary employees and the low waged end up being investment bankers suing for millions.
That's not exactly an unintended consequence, though. The government’s own analysis of the new law says that the effect of removing the cap will “have a positive, but limited, impact on households,” but that some groups “may particularly benefit, such as high-earning employees.” The hope is that it will reduce the complexity of cases and consequently reduce the burden on a heavily overloaded employment tribunal system. In other words, if bankers no longer feel the need to bring discrimination into the question, their unfair dismissal cases will be quicker and easier to decide.
Other experts, however, think that this might significantly increase the number of bankers bringing cases. In the current market, the calculation is still likely to favour the strategy of “suck it up and try to find another job quickly” for rainmakers, but for other ranks (and if thing get worse again), we might see a lot more challenges to whether the processes were all followed properly when a banker gets fired.
Elsewhere, there are few male bankers who haven’t daydreamed about the possibility of being played by Ryan Gosling in the film based on whatever eventual meltdown happens in their particular line of business. “Jared Vennett <https://www.theguardian.com/film/2016/jan/27/the-big-short-financially-accurate-adam-mckay-subprime-money-bale-gosling-pitt>”, the cheerful and cynical mortgage bond salesman allegedly based on Deutsche Bank’s Greg Lippmann, steals the show by seeing the financial crisis coming and profiting by selling collateralised loan obligations.
And now, anyone who thinks that private equity might be the next Big Short has the chance to take that same role with the development of “collateralised fund obligations <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-10/carlyle-plans-complex-credit-vehicle-to-kick-start-flagship-fund?taid=69b00bc1bee120000189c38b&utm_campaign=trueanthem&utm_content=business&utm_medium=social&utm_source=twitter&embedded-checkout=true>”. This market was launched last year with some flagship deals led by Goldman Sachs <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2025-10-17/vista-taps-goldman-to-bundle-fund-stakes-in-1-billion-deal>, but the forthcoming multi-billion dollar “Project Potomac”, refinancing some funds managed by Carlyle Group, looks like it is going to break new ground.
Obviously, the simple fact that someone is selling structured securities based on an asset class doesn’t necessarily mean anything in and of itself. To a large extent, it just shows that private equity is a mature and well established enough sector to have the kind of investor demand needed for deals like Project Potomac. But complicated and illiquid deals like this have a way of making big profits for the smartest bankers and traders, and a less happy history for less sophisticated players.
Meanwhile …
Bank of America has made some serious moves to build up its tech investment banking team. Jason Rowe has joined from Goldman Sachs to be co-head of global technology, former JPM banker Mahir Zaimoglu will be head of TMT M&A, Patrik Czornik is joining from JP Morgan to be co-head of TMT for the EMEA region and Gary Kirkham is rejoining from Centerview to be an executive vice-chair. So far, though, no reorganisation into “infrastructure” and “software” teams, as has been the fashion at other banks. (Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-10/bofa-hires-goldman-s-rowe-as-global-tech-co-head-in-tmt-push>)
Bill Ackman is having another try at an IPO of Pershing Square Capital (the last one having been cancelled in 2024), and that means a lot of new disclosure. Ackman’s distributions and profit share last year were $142.8m, while CIO Ryan Israel got $44m. (WSJ <https://www.wsj.com/finance/investing/bill-ackman-pershing-square-usa-files-for-ipo-7cdaaeb7?mod=mhp>)
Although a lot of the hiring activity in hedge funds has shifted to the Gulf region, Paris remains the place for quants. Even if the capital allocation decisions are made elsewhere, there is nowhere to rival the talent pool for “architects of models and infrastructure”. (Financial News <https://www.fnlondon.com/articles/why-paris-remains-a-quant-powerhouse-amid-middle-east-hedge-fund-rush-0900d03e?mod=topic_hedge-funds>)
Every lawyer’s worst stress nightmare is to have miscalculated the dates and file a crucial piece of paperwork a day late, derailing a multi-billion dollar M&A deal. Somebody at Simpson Thacher appears to have done just that for the Aramark/Entier deal, and presumably won’t be comforted by the judge’s remark that the damage done to the clients will be mitigated by being able to file a professional negligence claim. (FT <https://www.ft.com/content/7a4e22b5-7375-482c-bc6b-114d4c2bcb0b>)
Citi is hiring in Asia, and sees a good pipeline of financial sponsors deals in particular. (Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-10/citi-braces-for-busy-spell-for-financial-sponsors-deals-in-asia?utm_medium=email&utm_source=author_alert&utm_term=260310&utm_campaign=author_16891741&embedded-checkout=true>)
KPMG is promising “outsize monetary awards” which will be “considerably bigger” than the annual bonuses, for “AI Sparks” who can demonstrate that they’ve done something innovative with AI. (Business Insider <https://www.businessinsider.com/kpmg-ai-spark-awards-cash-prizes-for-employee-ai-innovation-2026-3>)

item.title: Turbulence hits banks' trading desks weeks after bonuses were paid
item.description:
If you've happily banked your bonus </news/banker-bonuses> for 2025, then congratulations. If you work for Nomura or Macquarie and are still waiting to be paid, then consolations. Much has changed in the past week.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Banks began this year raving about the possibilities for M&A deals </news/cant-get-an-m-and-a-job>in what was expected to be a "top decile" opportunity set; M&A </news/get-a-job-in-ma>bankers appear to have been paid bonuses </news/everything-we-know-about-2026-bonuses-by-bank> accordingly, with an eye to retention for the year ahead. Similarly, trading revenues were expected to be flat this year compared to last, and in areas like macro trading we understand that double digit bonus increases were recently forthcoming.
Just a few weeks later, banks may be wondering why they bothered.
Uncertainty created by war in the Middle East and rising oil prices is in danger of delaying M&A deals, which Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-06/dealmakers-see-iran-war-stymieing-m-a-with-delays-diligence>notes were already spooked by falling software stocks in February. Due diligence is taking longer, but it's hard to be definitive when oil prices could either fall or go through the roof. At the same time, macro hedge funds and commodities pods are thought to be nursing large losses, making it less likely that they'll poach banks' traders - who were paid to stay in place. And some of banks' own trading desks are understood to have made losses of their own.
Losses are thought to have afflicted businesses like UBS's SSA and covered bond trading desk </news/jonas-klink>, which was said to have been a driver of profits last year and to have been paid only recently commensurate with this. UBS declined to comment, as did the desk itself.
Speaking today at the RBC Global Financials conference, Citi CEO Jane Fraser said global economic growth will not be heavily impacted if oil remains above $100 for only four to six weeks. Large cap M&A is "not missing a beat" yet, said Fraser; there's more hesitation about mid-market M&A deals and smaller IPOs.
Equities and fixed income trading businesses are up by the mid-teens year-on-year but "obviously, that can change in 2.5 weeks," Fraser added. However, speaking at the same conference, Bank of America said <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-10/bofa-traders-expected-to-post-stronger-gains-from-volatility?utm_medium=email&utm_source=author_alert&utm_term=260310&utm_campaign=author_19908303> its trading business had benefitted from the volatility and should register a low double digit percentage increase in the first quarter.

item.title: SumUp employees who joined in 2020 might be sitting on millions ahead of IPO
item.description:
Fintech </news/how-to-get-a-job-in-fintech>IPOs aren't the hot topic they used to be but some firms are still intent on going public. SumUp, which provides payment </news/how-to-get-a-job-in-payments-banking-fintech> infrastructure, has been toying with the idea since last September; it's now inviting banks to pitch their services <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-10/european-fintech-sumup-said-to-seek-bankers-for-a-european-ipo> for a European IPO. Those who have been working at SumUp for some time might stand to make a lot of money from it.
Follow eFinancialCareers on Google News here. <https://news.google.com/publications/CAAqMggKIixDQklTR3dnTWFoY0tGV1ZtYVc1aGJtTnBZV3hqWVhKbFpYSnpMbU52YlNnQVAB?ceid=GB:en&oc=3>
SumUp is based in Luxembourg which means it doesn't have to release annual accounts, but it does so for its UK entities via Companies House. In accounts for 'SumUp Payments Limited', the firm breaks down how many stock options </news/joining-a-fintech-startup-for-the-stock-pay-is-a-dead-end> it granted to its employees and the price at which they were granted. Those working at SumUp in 2020 received their stock units for roughly 20 cents on the Euro.
SumUp was tight lipped <https://www.verdict.co.uk/sumup-funding/> about its valuation until it announced it was worth €8bn in 2022 <https://fstech.co.uk/fst/SumUp_Valued_At_8bn_After_590m_Fund_Raise.php>, but the firm said it had reached unicorn status by 2019. From 2020 to 2024, however, the average price of granted units in SumUp Payments Limited increased by more than 20x. Given that SumUp is eyeing a valuation of ~$10bn (€8.6bn), it seems those early employees got a particularly good deal. Stock pay in prior years is not disclosed but may be similarly discounted.
A price-per share for SumUp's IPO has yet to be touted. If we take 2024's average grant price as an estimate, the 16 employees who worked within that entity in 2020 would have been granted €4.4m, or €275k per head for that year's work alone.
There are caveats. For one, as this is not the parent entity, these shares would need to be swapped with SumUp shares, which may not occur at a 1:1 ratio. For two, not all of the stock was actually vested; from 2021 to 2024, over 233,000 stock units at SumUp Payments Limited either expired or were forfeited.
Then, there's the dreaded lockup period. Usually existing shareholders are prohibited from selling their stock until six months after the public offering, at which point the value may be up... or down. We noted this after Figma went public last August </news/ex-barclays-and-morgan-stanley-bankers-holding-millions-at-figma-face-an-awkward-5-months>; when its lockup period ended in February, the price was less than a quarter of its peak. SumUp staff will hope this fate doesn't await them.

item.title: Goldman Sachs' ex-MDs at Balyasny are on the move after a heavy week
item.description:
If you leave an investment bank for a hedge fund </news/2022/07/leave-banking-for-a-hedge-fund>, you must bear the consequences. Sometimes things go well. Sometimes they do not. And if they do not, patience can suddenly wear thin.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
It is not clear whether thinning patience was behind this week's unexpected departure of Damien Courvalin from hedge fund Balyasny Asset Management (BAM). BAM itself isn't commenting and Courvalin didn't respond to a request to explain his exit. His mysterious egress is a source of curiosity in the market given both BAM's reported <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-10/balyasny-millennium-point72-hit-by-iran-conflict-in-wild-week?utm_medium=email&utm_source=author_alert&utm_term=260310&utm_campaign=author_19458291> 3.5% loss during the week through to March 6, and Courvalin's own pedigree.
Courvalin is something of a star. He joined Balyasny Asset Management in 2023 to lead a 'Commodities Intelligence Group' as the hedge fund built out its commodities business. Courvalin previously spent 16 years at Goldman Sachs in New York as the firm's combined head of energy research and a strategist covering everything from precious metals to agricultural research and commodities.
However, Courvalin wasn't BAM's only ex-Goldman guy. One year before he arrived there, BAM also hired another Goldman MD in the form of Dan Deighton who became its overall head of global commodities. Deighton himself spent 12 years at Goldman Sachs and was the firm's head of commodity index and agricultural products trading. At BAM, Courvalin reported to Deighton, even though Deighton's star was arguably less shiny than his own.
Now Courvalin is out and Deighton remains in his seat at BAM. It's not clear what's become of the fund's other ex-Goldman hires, including Callum Andrew Bruce who only joined Courvalin's team in November last year. Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-09/balyasny-has-two-europe-energy-traders-leave-in-volatile-week> reported yesterday that two other energy traders - Toby Sheppard and Max Iakovlev - also left BAM this week for reasons unknown. Iakovlev is another ex-Goldman MD.
Where will Courvalin and Iakovlev go next? Other hedge funds might like to hear from them; so too might their previous employer. They're unlikely to be the only hedge fund professionals on the move: it's been a difficult month </news/hedge-fund-job-fears-after-tempestuous-week>, particularly in macro. "We're all down 5%," says the head of one top fund. "I don't think we will all make that back," he adds.

item.title: Hedge fund Qube Research hired Hudson River Trading's head of options market making
item.description:
Qube Research and Technologies </news/qube-research-and-technologies-pay-quant-hedge-fund> (QRT) may not be growing quite as fast as its meteoric rise in 2024, but it's still hiring some big names. The latest addition in the hedge fund </news/how-to-get-a-hedge-fund-job>'s market making division is a quant </news/quant-jobs-finance>with a quarter century of experience.
Follow eFinancialCareers on Google News here. <https://news.google.com/publications/CAAqMggKIixDQklTR3dnTWFoY0tGV1ZtYVc1aGJtTnBZV3hqWVhKbFpYSnpMbU52YlNnQVAB?ceid=GB:en&oc=3>
Beat Ammon joined Qube as its head of research for options market making. He was most recently an algo developer at Hudson River Trading </news/high-frequency-trading-hiring-and-pay> in New York, <https://brokercheck.finra.org/individual/summary/4589084#:~:text=LP%20(CRD%23%3A152144)-,New%20York%2C%20NY,-B> where he spent four years as its head of options market making. Qube has been expanding into the US, with planned offices in Chicago, New York <https://www.linkedin.com/posts/2026-is-off-to-a-strong-start-were-now-share-7416437559230361600-TX6w/> and Houston <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2025-07-15/qube-to-enter-us-after-building-30-billion-hedge-fund-empire>, but Ammon has been relocated to Qube's London HQ for the role.
Ammon also spent a decade as a Morgan Stanley MD in its automated options market making team, and was previously head of volatility arbitrage quant research at hedge fund Citadel. He worked in market making technology as early as 2001 with Credit Suisse.
Electronic market making isn't that common among hedge funds, given the need for cutting edge low latency technology. Instead, trading firms like Jane Street and Citadel Securities dominate the space. Qube employs far more technologists than your average hedge fund, however, and has multiple cultural similarities </news/qrt-hedge-fund> to firms like Jane Street. </news/how-to-get-a-job-at-jane-street> Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2025-02-13/how-secretive-hedge-fund-qrt-hit-the-big-time> said last year that it has been able to generate hundreds of millions of dollars by making markets.
Qube did not respond to a request for comment.

item.title: Bankers in Paris are ready to return to London as tax deals run out
item.description:
London as a destination for banking and trading jobs may be having a moment. Not only has the UAE's star dimmed, but the tax advantages that made Paris popular are wearing thin.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
As we have written here before </news/2022/07/expatriate-tax-regime-bankers-france>, France's "inpatriates" tax regime has been good for the thousands of formerly London-based bankers who moved to Paris after Brexit. However, the tax advantages afforded by the scheme only last for eight years, and for many senior bankers and traders in Paris, that time is nearly up.
"I've started to get people calling me from Paris to ask about a return to London," says one macro headhunter. It's more trickle than flood. But it's noticeable. Senior traders in London tell us they're getting calls from Paris-based traders too.
France introduced its inpatriates regime in 2008. It offers tax incentives to people moving to France who haven't lived and worked there for five years previously, French nationals included. The regime is complex, <https://www.paris-europlace.com/global/gene/link.php?doc_id=21333&fg=1>but effectively allows for individuals to receive 30% or more of their salary or bonus tax-free and to reduce capital gains tax. Beneficiaries can nominate a proportion of their work as being carried out abroad and pay no tax on that portion. And their foreign-based assets are exempt from French wealth tax.
Without the inpatriates regime, bankers and traders in France will need to pay to pay a wealth tax of between 0.5% and 1.5% a year on their assets above €1.3m. They will also need to pay marginal tax rates that can be as high as 55% once the French surcharge on incomes above €250k is factored in.
This is why, as the eight year deadline draws near, people are exploring their options. Although Brexit officially took place in 2020, many traders moved to Paris before that. Bank of America, for example, expanded </news/2019/05/bank-of-america-paris> its Paris office dramatically in 2019. JPMorgan and Goldman Sachs </news/2020/09/pay-bankers-paris-frankfurt-brexit>moved people to Paris from 2020 onwards.
The staggered relocation of staff initially means tax-inspired exits from Paris will be a dribble rather than a deluge. London may not be the only beneficiary. A post-war Dubai and Abu Dhabi could regain their lustre. Alternatively, there's always Singapore and Hong Kong.
All are possible for traders in banks. Portfolio managers in Paris remain few and far between. "Barely any hedge funds moved there," observes one MD. "- And why would you want to live in Paris anyway?"

item.title: London’s macro traders could earn over $800k, one day
item.description:
If you’re a young macro specialist, perhaps working at a bank </news/the-investment-banking-recruitment-process>, hedge fund </news/how-to-get-a-hedge-fund-job>, or asset management </news/how-to-get-a-job-in-asset-management> firm, you might be wondering where the place for you to work is. Fortunately, the decision is an easy one to make.
Click here to join the bubble by eFinancialCareers, our new anonymous community. <https://bubble.efinancialcareers.com/login?_gl=1*ut8n6u*_gcl_au*MTg4MDY2NTExNi4xNzYxNTU3NDIz*_ga*ODczNzI2NTA0LjE3NTM2OTk2ODI.*_ga_NZFQV4MXPM*czE3NjM2NDM0MDYkbzI1NiRnMSR0MTc2MzY0MzQzOSRqMjckbDAkaDMyMjc4OTUzMw..>✍️
A salary survey from recruitment firm Octavius Finance sheds light on the matter. It says the best paid macro professionals are to be found in hedge funds – at all levels.
At the highest level, compensation packages are particularly eye watering. Professionals with over 10 years of experience can expect to earn a minimum of £225k ($302k) in salaries and bonuses at that point, or a maximum above £600k ($807k). People in this category are probably portfolio managers; their compensation is tied to the profit and loss (PnL) they generate.
If you don't want to be a macro portfolio manager with a hedge fund, there are alternatives. Octavius says sell-side fixed income professionals (at this point, senior traders) can earn over £500k ($672k). Asset management PMs lag behind.
If you want to work in a hedge fund after all, which is the best one to go to? Octavius Finance says that it was multi-strategy hedge funds that are “a major draw for macro strategy talent,.” Those roles “often win competitive hiring processes when compared with sell-side opportunities.”

item.title: DRW hired an ex-Goldman Sachs MD that replicated strategies of trading firms
item.description:
Quantitative </news/quant-jobs-finance> Investment Strategies (QIS) teams in banks make money in multiple ways, most infamously by replicating established strategies from hedge funds </news/how-to-get-a-hedge-fund-job> and trading firms </news/high-frequency-trading-hiring-and-pay>. A Goldman Sachs MD who spent 13 years in QIS no longer has to watch from outside.
Follow eFinancialCareers on Google News here. <https://news.google.com/publications/CAAqMggKIixDQklTR3dnTWFoY0tGV1ZtYVc1aGJtTnBZV3hqWVhKbFpYSnpMbU52YlNnQVAB?ceid=GB:en&oc=3>
Javier Rodriguez-Alarcon has joined Don Wilson's electronic trading firm DRW as a London-based managing director. According to its most recent accounts via Companies House (for 2024), DRW has over 300 people in the UK and pays over $320k on average. Alarcon joins from XBTO, a digital asset prop trading firm where he was CIO, but spent the bulk of his career at Goldman Sachs.
For 13 years, Rodriguez-Alarcon was EMEA head of the bank's quantitative investment strategies (QIS) team. These teams replicate trading strategies of hedge funds and trading firms, but are packaged as swaps or structured notes to make them more attractive to banking clients. At the time Rodriguez-Alarcon left Goldman in 2023, Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2023-05-19/wall-street-built-a-370-billion-business-cloning-quant-trades> reported that QIS trades had a volume of $370bn. Goldman's QIS team was multifaceted; the FT <https://www.ft.com/content/fdfd5e78-0283-11e7-aa5b-6bb07f5c8e12>reported in 2017 that it also provided tax-efficient structured products and had a 'smart beta' business investing in specific market factors or characteristics.
Interestingly, SocGen said last year <https://www.structuredretailproducts.com/insights/81239/sg-qis-is-being-adopted-by-the-hedge-fund-industry> that its own QIS strategies are becoming popular not just with pension funds and larger clients, but with the hedge funds whose strategies are being copied.

item.title: Ex-Goldman Sachs banker that worked on thousands of financial models wants AI to build it for you
item.description:
According to Anthropic, financial services is very exposed to AI in theory, but hardly affected in practice. </news/ai-hedge-fund-jobs-vs-banking> An ex-Goldman Sachs </news/how-to-get-a-job-goldman-sachs> executive director is looking to change that.
Follow eFinancialCareers on Google News here. <https://news.google.com/publications/CAAqMggKIixDQklTR3dnTWFoY0tGV1ZtYVc1aGJtTnBZV3hqWVhKbFpYSnpMbU52YlNnQVAB?ceid=GB:en&oc=3>
Mo Achour spent eight years working in M&A </news/what-are-ma-jobs> at Goldman Sachs in London. He left last year to found Axe AI, which 'builds institutional-grade financial models in minutes, not weeks'. While AI has been effective for word-based tasks, Achour said "Excel is a different beast; it’s cells communicating with each other and formulas inside those cells."
Financial services professionals today don't seem to be impressed with the offerings from established AI labs like Anthropic and OpenAI </news/openai-culture>. When Claude for Excel released, hedge fund employees said </news/bankers-frustrated-with-ai-liken-their-jobs-to-manual-labour> it was "far from ready for institutional use cases" at best and "a complete and utter mess" at worst, thanks to its propensity for hallucinations. </news/the-other-problem-goldman-sachs-faces-with-ll-ms>
In banking, Achour said "there’s no such thing as good enough. It has to be perfect."
Building an AI startup is a risky endeavour. An MIT report last year found that 95% of GenAI projects fail, and basic functionality from the major bots has only gotten better since then. Indeed, OpenAI is encroaching on Achour's turf with 'Project Mercury,' </news/open-ai-junior-bankers>where junior bankers are paid $150 per hour to train OpenAI's LLMs on financial modelling. Achour said his edge is that Axe gets "to skip that entire learning phase," since he "built and reviewed thousands of models" while at Goldman. He said it's not just about knowing what makes a model robust, but also about "what makes it flexible when an MD changes the brief at midnight." </news/2023/10/banking-internship>
Unlike most investment bankers building startups, Achour has an engineering background via the French Grandes Ecoles </news/2021/09/french-students-banking-jobs>, which has allowed him to "see problems and solutions in a different manner." He said being "bilingual" is a rare trait today; "there's not a lot of people that understand tech and finance." That may change if the likes of Project Mercury continue.

item.title: Morning Coffee: The many ways of earning $200k+ that don't involve banking. Wild Asian hiring
item.description:
When you're in financial services, it can seem that you work very hard and earn a lot of money and that there are few other options. We intermittently remind </news/2009/01/top-20-best-paid-jobs-outside-banking>you that this is not the case, and now NYMag <https://nymag.com/intelligencer/article/new-york-salaries-jobs.html?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=s1&utm_campaign=nym>has done the same. It turns out that there are many, many jobs that pay well. They are not all what you would think.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
NYMag spoke to 53 people in New York City about their pay checks. Their information is extracted and inserted into the chart below. While exterminating lice, working in an Amazon warehouse or helping elderly people pay badly, there are many roles that pay well. It turns out that even dog walkers in Manhattan are on $90k.
If you're a junior banker complaining about hours </news/i-worked-110-hours-a-week-as-a-junior-banker-in-london-and-then-quit>, there are therefore alternatives. They involve hard work too: you might earn $462k as a publicist, but the publicist earning this says "it's a seven-days-a-week-hustle" involving "every hot restaurant." Choose your grind.
Separately, while the war might have upset </news/hedge-fund-job-fears-after-tempestuous-week>hedge fund hiring and shunted many bankers into endless rounds of interviews with no outcome, it has not unsettled hiring in Asia.
Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-10/senior-bankers-quit-for-rivals-as-asia-talent-fight-intensifies?utm_medium=email&utm_source=author_alert&utm_term=260310&utm_campaign=author_3492388> reports that the fight over senior bankers in Asia is "intensifying." It points to six senior banker moves as evidence of this. Citi, for example has hired someone (Indran Thana from UBS) to replace someone (Jonathan Quek, who went to Jefferies). Warren Wu, head of TMT for South East Asia and India at UBS resigned. So did Karen Chen, JPMorgan's China head of consumer and retail.
The intensification follows a report from headhunting firm Maven Partners which said there were 105 banking MD moves in APAC investment banking last year and a 100% "surge" in moves to competing banks. Hong Kong was the main source of action.
Meanwhile...
Hedge funds had a terrible week last week. Millennium lost about $1.5bn in the week through March 6; Balyasny was down 3.5%. Blame the war. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-10/balyasny-millennium-point72-hit-by-iran-conflict-in-wild-week?utm_medium=email&utm_source=author_alert&utm_term=260310&utm_campaign=author_19458291>
“We are in full-blown panic unwind mode, with price action across cross-regional stocks illustrating extreme levels of stress..." Things may be looking up though. Barclays says these kinds of troughs are usually followed by a rebound. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-09/-full-blown-panic-unwind-in-momentum-trade-is-signaling-a-nadir?taid=69ae9c945670740001587eeb&utm_campaign=trueanthem&utm_content=business&utm_medium=social&utm_source=twitter&sref=DK3y4h9m>
Goldman Sachs, Morgan Stanley and Citi are letting UAE staff work from elsewhere if they like. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-09/iran-war-wall-street-banks-offer-uae-staff-option-to-relocate-temporarily?fromMostRead=true>
Goldman Sachs has has offered clients complex trades that would allow them to profit from further falls in loans made to software companies. (Financial Times) <https://www.ft.com/content/52c4f129-891f-4afe-9cf4-3934630a50ad>
Mike Lamb left Citi last year and now he's back at Barclays as chair within its European financial institutions group. (Financial News) < https://www.fnlondon.com/articles/ex-citigroup-dealmaker-lamb-re-joins-barclays-as-chair-of-fig-5e34a8c0?mod=homepage>
Deutsche Bank's bonus pool was probably up 5%. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-09/deutsche-bank-is-said-to-raise-its-bonus-pool-by-more-than-5?srnd=phx-industries-finance>
A senior executive at Odey Asset Management says he felt physically threatened by Crispin Odey after advising that Odey work from home in light of the harassment allegations against him. "I have . . . never seen behaviour like this in the course of my professional life, or indeed my personal life.” (Financial Times) <. https://www.ft.com/content/4b617195-a6c8-43e8-bbd4-4014df6239b7>
Is it really necessary to work 100 hour weeks in banking? “A huge part of the hours are artificially created. Seniors create fake urgency and have juniors stay until 4am to finish a pitchbook for a meeting that isn’t even scheduled yet. You’ll spend hours building a page and a senior will change their mind and delete it the next morning.” (Financial News) <https://www.fnlondon.com/articles/banking-broke-me-why-centerviews-sleep-case-hasnt-put-junior-burnout-to-bed-c0602f9b?mod=homepage>
446 employees out of 5,700 will leave the Bank of England and they will each receive an average of £81k. It's a "mutually agreed resignation scheme." (Bloomberg) < https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-09/almost-1-in-10-bank-of-england-staff-to-leave-under-savings-push?srnd=homepage-uk>
Receiving extra vacation time rather than bonus money makes workers feel valued as individuals with a life beyond work. (WSJ) <https://www.wsj.com/lifestyle/careers/employee-appreciation-cash-bonus-vacation-time-c404c632?mod=hp_jr_pos3>
If want to impress Trump you need to wear $145 loafters by Florsheim, an American brand combining comfort and style. (WSJ) <https://www.wsj.com/style/fashion/trump-florsheim-shoes-tucker-carlson-jd-vance-bessent-448567ab?mod=hp_lead_pos8>

item.title: Barclays probably lost $700m to MFS. It also employed the owner's son as a credit trader
item.description:
At this stage in the proceedings, Barclays undoubtedly wishes it had never heard of Market Financial Solutions (MFS), the British non-bank that has gone into administration owing it $700m. And yet it was linked to MFS in more ways than one.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Barclays not only leant money to MFS through its asset backed lending business, it also provided banking services to MFS, and it employed the founder's son.
Rihan Dewan </news/deutsche-bank-poached-a-barclays-high-yield-trader-amidst-musical-chairs>, the high yield credit trader who moved from Barclays to Deutsche Bank in September last year, is understood to be the son of Tiba Raja and the son in law of Paresh Raja, who founded MFS in 2006.
Dewan spent just after three years at Barclays after joining from HSBC in 2022. He graduated from the University of Warwick in 2017.
There is no indication that Dewan was involved in any wrongdoing at Barclays or at MFS, but insiders aware of his parentage have been noting the coincidence. The Financial Times <https://www.ft.com/content/4f1e97ac-57a5-4e06-91bb-537e270217d7>reported last week that Barclays began blocking some transactions relating to MFS months before it froze all accounts there in early January.
Dewan didn't respond to a request to comment. Barclays declined to comment.
Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-05/mfs-ceo-spent-on-artwork-parties-indian-rapper-before-collapse>reported that Tiba and Paresh Raja spent their time moving between London, Monaco and Dubai and threw legendary parties for MFS staff, sometimes costing hundreds of millions of pounds. Last week, the Wall Street Journal <https://www.wsj.com/finance/how-a-british-mortgage-company-became-private-credits-latest-black-eye-1d93f342?gaa_at=eafs&gaa_n=AWEtsqdhq2pMIBX8Vy7H5WoNkeVh-Ww18s91640c1Aae_CczJ1R03YDzCrGiV42QpbU%3D&gaa_ts=69af0a9a&gaa_sig=DMIDhfKPwhfO_2jfSt4igMOoaOzlQISNreqOu-dKMTEM4L48iBZlOvtsvsI18qs6FCCPjZ3UUDhSjciqF4F6nw%3D%3D>reported that Tiba and Paresh were holed up in Dubai. Paresh's maiden name is Dewan.
Barclays is likely to have made the loans to MFS through its investment bank. As a trader, Rihan Dewan was not involved. Some have suggested a conflict of interest simply because Rihan Dewan worked for Barclays, but this is not understood to have been the case.
Barclays' share price has fallen nearly 20% in the past month. Fortunately, Rihan Dewan's bonus will now be denominated in Deutsche Bank stock which is down only 16% over the same period.
Barclays rarely sounds exuberant about its investment bank. During its investor call last month, the bank explained that its prioritizing corporate banking over investment banking and said that it expects revenue growth in the business to slow in the next three years.

item.title: When you lose your job at UBS, you will probably receive $125k
item.description:
UBS is not done with cutting costs. As we reported early in February, the Swiss bank wants to extract another $1.1bn </news/ubs-will-be-cutting-another-1-1bn-from-employment-costs-this-year>from employment costs in 2025. Given that the average of UBS's 105,326 people earned $265k last year, this implies there are over 4,000 more job cuts to come, soon.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
If you are afflicted, the good news is that you can at least expect a six figure payout.
UBS's 2025 annual report <https://www.ubs.com/content/dam/assets/cc/investor-relations/annual-report/2025/annual-report-ubs-group-2025.pdf>, released today, shows that the bank spent $942m on severance payments for 7,538 people last year, or an average of $125k for each unwanted employee. This was strangely consistent with the average severance payment of $129k that UBS gave the 5,696 people it chopped in 2024.
The key risk takers fired from UBS last year were predictably paid a bit more to disappear. 19 people in this category vanished in 2025 and they received an average of $263k each to do so. This looks parsimonious to the $500k HSBC paid its senior bankers to go, though.
UBS's annual report also reveals that it made 8,024 external hires in 2025. This was down only slightly from the 8,525 external hires that occurred in 2024, but a substantial decline from the 11,435 people UBS recruited from elsewhere in the zealous days of 2023.
The number of graduates, interns and apprentices hired into the bank fell 43% in just two years, to 2,117 in 2025.

item.title: A low-key electronic market maker bumped pay to $440k
item.description:
While private firms like Citadel Securities and Jane Street </news/citadel-securities-vs-jane-street> dominate the conversation of electronic market making </news/high-frequency-trading-hiring-and-pay#:~:text=Electronic%20Market%20Making,hedge%20their%20risk.>, publicly traded Marex has been going under the radar. It had its best ever quarter for revenues to close out last year, and bumped up pay in spite of an influx of new hires.
Follow eFinancialCareers on Google News here. <https://news.google.com/publications/CAAqMggKIixDQklTR3dnTWFoY0tGV1ZtYVc1aGJtTnBZV3hqWVhKbFpYSnpMbU52YlNnQVAB?ceid=GB:en&oc=3>
Marex increased average headcount from 2,334 in 2024 to 2,786 in 2025. It made just under 300 hires in the back office and just over 150 hires in the front. Despite this, 'salaries and wages' per head were up 6.5% to $443k. Jane Street and Citadel Securities, by contrast, each paid over $2m per head to their employees last year. </news/jane-street-versus-citadel-securities-pay>
Various acquisitions made by Marex influenced its growth. Speaking in the firm's earnings call, CFO Rob Irvin said Marex's compensation expense was up due to "higher front-office compensation and the additional headcount following the Winterflood acquisition;" Marex bought the equities market maker for over £100m ($134m) last summer. It also acquired FX services firm Hamilton Court earlier in the year. At the end of the year it had over 3,100 employees.
Marex made multiple big hires last year. In October it hired senior Jefferies trader Aaron Fernandes to build out an emerging markets credit desk. It hired Darren Williams as its global head of relative value trading in December following a legal battle <https://www.fnlondon.com/articles/lawsuits-infighting-and-a-16m-man-how-city-broker-jb-drax-soured-on-its-star-traders-16c9f5fb>between him and his former employer JB Drax.
Although Marex pays less than a quarter of some of its rivals, there are benefits to working there. Insiders have told us </news/marex-recruitment> off the record that Marex "a breath of fresh air" full of ambitious people, and that it operates a "no jerk" rule.
