item.title: Terraform Labs staff really wanted to work for Jane Street
item.description:
Many people want to work at Jane Street. At blockchain infrastructure firm Terraform, the lab behind TerraUSD, multiple people were interested in joining the electronic trading giant. It seems that only one - Terraform intern Bryce Pratt - succeeded in doing so.
Follow eFinancialCareers on Google News here. <https://news.google.com/publications/CAAqMggKIixDQklTR3dnTWFoY0tGV1ZtYVc1aGJtTnBZV3hqWVhKbFpYSnpMbU52YlNnQVAB?ceid=GB:en&oc=3>
A filing <https://www.scribd.com/document/1002819113/Jane-Street-Lawsuit#content=query:telegram,pageNum:21,indexOnPage:0,bestMatch:false> this week in the New York state courts accuses Jane Street of "exploit[ing]" crypto markets and "contribut[ing] to the collapse of Terraform's cryptocurrency ecosystem. </news/2022/05/bitcoin-bros>" Central to the filing is Bryce Pratt, who interned at Terraform before joining Jane Street in September 2021. Pratt allegedly leaked confidential information to Jane Street via the infamous "Bryce's Secret" group chat. The filing notes that at least two of his senior coworkers were interested in working at Jane Street with him.
One of them, Terraform's unnamed head of research was reportedly "actively seeking" to be hired by Jane Street </news/jane-street-s-traders-on-1-4m-have-fighter-pilot-eyes-high-pain-thresholds> and interviewed there in March 2022. Two months later, this researcher was also sending messages via Telegram to Pratt. In those messages, Pratt allegedly gave the research head a "glimpse into Jane Street." This information exchange was said to be a "two-way street."
The filing also alleges that Pratt had a "close friendship" with a second Terraform employee, and the pair "discussed the possibility of working together at Jane Street in the future." Pratt seems to have been popular during his brief period as a Terraform intern: even after leaving, the filing says he "maintained close relationships with many [Terraform] employees."
This might have been partly because Pratt was already working in Jane Street's promised land. Jane Street paid $1.4m per head in 2024 </news/jane-street-s-1-4bn-pay-is-less-communist-than-2005-goldman-sachs>. It's not clear exactly how much Terraform paid prior to its bankruptcy, but previous reports <https://cryptorank.io/news/feed/8d700-outrage-as-terraform-labs-ceo-receives-8-million-while-investors-suffer> suggest it had a $6.8m pool of 'key employee retention plan awards' for senior staff. ~$4.5m went to Terraform's CEO, so it's possible the research head's package was less than even the average Jane Street employee.
It's not easy to get a job at Jane Street, which in the past has been known to conduct interviews </news/electronic-trading-interviews> involving ruthlessly competitive card games. </news/2023/10/jane-street-interview-process-sam-bankman-fried> Jane Street likes to hire at graduate level </news/the-20-universities-jane-street-loves-most-for-its-quants-and-engineers>, which might explain why Pratt the intern got in and the head of research - who was a "friend and mentor" to him according to the filing, did not.
We asked Jane Street to comment on whether the Terraform research head ever interviewed or worked at the firm. A spokesperson for Jane Street said “this desperate suit is a transparent attempt to extract money when it is well-established that the losses suffered by Terra and Luna holders were the result of a multi-billion dollar fraud perpetrated by the management of Terraform Labs. We will defend ourselves vigorously against these baseless, opportunistic claims.” We have not spoken to Pratt.

item.title: "AI is not going to get rid of 18 hour days for investment bank analysts."
item.description:
I've spent years working in M&A </news/what-are-ma-jobs>at a top US bank. Thanks to AI </news/how-to-get-a-banking-ai-job>, things that used to take analysts multiple weeks will soon take a matter of hours. This doesn't mean investment banking will suddenly become a 9 to 5.
Follow eFinancialCareers on Google News here. <https://news.google.com/publications/CAAqMggKIixDQklTR3dnTWFoY0tGV1ZtYVc1aGJtTnBZV3hqWVhKbFpYSnpMbU52YlNnQVAB?ceid=GB:en&oc=3>
Those long hours </news/best-lifestyle-finance> are simply how the industry works and how you're trained. There will always be more things to do and more clients to win. If you're a junior working in finance, you naturally want to do more than everyone else, to win deals and work on profitable desks. That means working 18 hour days </news/junior-hedge-fund-analyst-working-hours>, with or without AI.
Managing directors </news/banks-weird-hierarchies-analysts-associates-vps-mds>(MDs) will expect more of you when this work becomes easier too. They'll want to hold more meetings with clients and they'll ask you to conduct much more in-depth analysis. All this means for them is that a meeting that once took 10 days to schedule can now take two or three days; they'll expect the same output.
That's not to say conditions won't improve. Hours </news/i-worked-110-hours-a-week-as-a-junior-banker-in-london-and-then-quit> will get slightly better, but not dramatically. I just don't see a world where analysts will uniformly finish at 8pm.
Said Kamali is a pseudonym.

item.title: Morning Coffee: Goldman Sachs partners keep disappearing post bonuses. How to get paid well but perform not so well at HSBC
item.description:
It's a partner year </news/goldman-sachs-partners-list-2024> at Goldman Sachs. This means that before new partners appear, old partners leave to make space for them. Some of Goldman's most gnarly old partners have gone this week, but it's not clear whether the firm would have wished their departures.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
The first exit was that of Nikhil Choraria </news/nikhil-choraria>, who ran an inflation trading desk that made $450m in 2021 and who has vanished into the ether. </news/citi-s-top-inflation-trader-is-joining-brevan-howard-goldman-s-is-a-man-of-mystery>Now, the Financial Times <https://www.ft.com/content/6554d91e-c2b7-4510-9db7-2ac4bd62469e> reports that Erdit Hoxha, Goldman's equities co-head is also leaving the firm for hedge fund Millennium. </news/how-to-get-a-job-at-hedge-fund-millennium>Goldman only promoted Hoxha as its equities co-head in 2025, so his departure does not seem felicitous.
Both men had been at Goldman for 22 years, having joined in the same 2004 analyst class. Choraria was promoted to MD in 2012 and then to partner in 2018. Hoxha made MD in 2013 and partner in 2016.
Both men have enough money to never work again. Goldman Sachs partners receive salaries of $950k, </news/goldman-sachs-partners> plus bonuses, plus profit shares, plus opportunities to coinvest in Goldman's own funds. Choraria also presumably received a particularly large bonus for his good work in 2021.
Hoxha, at least, would like a bit more. Millennium is known for its large packages. Given that Hoxha is joining Millennium's office of the chief investment officer, his package there may be larger than most.
43-year-old Hoxha has come a long way in life. Born in Tirana, the capital of Albania, he completed a finance degree at Bocconi, known for populating London with suave Italian financiers. Hoxha, who shares a surname with Albania's former communist dictator, has long been a proponent of meritocracy and of Albanians in finance. Another Hoxha, who may or may not be his wife, is a top fixed income fund manager.
The good news for Goldman is that it now has two empty partner spots to fill and that the potential candidates will work hard all year in the hope of being promoted in November. The bad news is that Millennium </news/2023/11/goldman-sachs-to-hedge-funds-millennium-and-citadel> has yet another senior Goldman Sachs trader on its team. Other Goldman partners with itchy feet know exactly who will hire them.
Separately, as we reported yesterday </news/hey-hsbc-s-us-bonuses-were-really-great-actually>recipients of HSBC's bonuses </news/hsbc-is-paying-senior-bankers-who-stay-1-4m-and-senior-bankers-who-go-500k> in New York seem pleased with their lot and are expressing surprise at the bank's generosity.
The Financial Times <https://www.ft.com/content/9c02b970-b704-42bd-abb9-f096bfd7fe96> reports that HSBC's non-executive directors have also been handsomely remunerated, despite flailing around trying to find a new chairman and eventually promoting a stop-gap internal chair before Christmas.
Independent director Ann Godbehere did particularly well from HSBC's pay-pot. The FT notes that she had her fees doubled £737k last year, despite leading the failed chairman search and leaving shortly after the stop-gap candidate was appointed. Godbehere was paid partly for sitting on some new committees, including the remuneration committee which decides remuneration.
Meanwhile...
Beatriz Martin, head of the central technology division at UBS, is upsetting people by cutting technology jobs. (Inside Paradeplatz) <https://insideparadeplatz.ch/2026/02/26/ubs-shooting-woman-zerschlaegt-die-informatik/>
Block is cutting 40% of its workforce. Jack Dorsey says: "Within the next year, I believe the majority of companies will reach the same conclusion and make similar structural changes. I’d rather get there honestly and on our own terms than be forced into it reactively.” (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-02-26/jack-dorsey-s-block-slashes-nearly-half-of-workforce-in-ai-bet>
There are ego issues at Blue Owl. Michael Rees, the founder of Dyal Capital (one of three entities that formed Blue Owl in 2021) fell out with the firm's other co-founders after its creation and was then sidelined in 2023 when two others were named as co-chief executives and he stayed as co-president. (FT) <https://www.ft.com/content/f2deb825-86d3-4b5d-805b-aee77e0b8384>
Dispersion trades are all the rage. They involve buying single-stock volatility while selling index volatility to profit from the gap between the S&P 500 low volatility and large price swings for individual companies. This works well, unless stocks fall in unison, at which point investors who have bet on dispersion might be forced to buy index-level volatility protection, which would worsen a market sell-off. (FT) <https://www.ft.com/content/dede31ed-6b85-4e12-b9c9-3bfbd4449d75>
Portfolio manager Chiara Chabanne and her husband and analyst Antoine Chabanne moved together </news/2023/06/husband-and-wife-hedge-fund>to work for Hudson Capital Management in Dubai. Now they're moving togeher again, to work for Dymon Asia Capital. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-02-26/hudson-bay-s-husband-wife-trading-duo-in-dubai-exit-for-dymon?utm_medium=email&utm_source=author_alert&utm_term=260227&utm_campaign=author_19458291>
It's been a great quarter for Canadian banks that report before the rest. CIBC had “record revenue across all of our business units and higher return on equity.” (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-02-26/cibc-earnings-top-estimates-on-record-revenue-across-divisions?srnd=phx-industries-finance>
ECB employees in Frankfurt are unhappy. Only 40% say they're satisified with promotion opportunities. More than 33% say they can't express their views without fear of negative consequences. No one is being hired; no one is leaving. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-02-26/lagarde-finds-ecb-staff-disillusioned-by-lack-of-opportunities?taid=69a0563c6a41fe0001bda188&utm_campaign=trueanthem&utm_content=business&utm_medium=social&utm_source=twitter>
Howard Lutnick has a habit of jumping into complex projects and claiming the win and this is creating resentment among other officials. (Politico) <https://www.politico.com/news/2026/02/26/howard-lutnick-trumps-dealmaker-tensions-00798716>
Investors have been buying rights to tariff refunds from companies that can't be bothered to pursue them.“The question is, do you sell at 40% or 45% now, or a little more if the market changes, or do you hold out for the 100% down the road?" (WSJ) <https://www.wsj.com/finance/wall-street-traders-are-pouncing-on-the-tariff-refund-chaos-d0144703?mod=hp_listb_pos4>
42 year-old fund manager Andrew Axelrod owns the biggest electronic dance music club in New York. It's been complicated. (WSJ) <https://www.wsj.com/finance/mirage-brooklyn-axar-axelrod-club-77a70b77>
AI executives are advising their children to work in nuclear energy. (WSJ) <https://www.wsj.com/lifestyle/careers/what-ai-executives-tell-their-own-kids-about-the-jobs-of-the-future-1ba43f65?mod=e2tw>
Do not say bandwidth, circle back, deep dive, or growth mindset. (WSJ) <https://www.wsj.com/business/c-suite/corporate-jargon-definitions-bebceb48?mod=hp_jr_pos1>

item.title: Hey, HSBC's US bonuses were really great actually!
item.description:
If you were worried about HSBC bonuses, well so were we. It turns out that concerns were at least partly misplaced though.
People working for HSBC's US debt capital markets </news/capital-markets-jobs-ecm-and-dcm>(DCM) business in particular, seem to have received fine bonuses for last year. We understand that the increases were substantial. Bonuses were high to the point of astonishment.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
HSBC's declined to comment in any detail on this show of generosity. A spokesman for the bank said: “We are committed to attracting and retaining talent as part of HSBC’s high-performance culture, ensuring our employees are rewarded competitively.”
Data from the London Stock Exchange Group <https://www.wsj.com/investment-banking-scorecard?gaa_at=eafs&gaa_n=AWEtsqeSHJ_Q5MgrS5oygiDKondF0pZYsj78ZMOwKlPAHOu-_nURmZa-lOwBuysYpV0%3D&gaa_ts=69a096c7&gaa_sig=hgEoE88V7Sze1kzLbLOeSUcA7FK9jhQy7px72MVNymmKNlDunR8jolpSgia2OCBACk4FBdFY5otC82BYTf62Dg%3D%3D#/dcm/us/currentyear>says HSBC's DCM business ranked 12th in the US for the first two months of 2025, but is up to 10th for the same period of this year.
The US DCM business has lost many people in the past few years, with insiders claiming that there have been 30+ exits. </news/hsbc-fired-more-bankers-before-bonuses-it-s-harsh-and-inhumane> More recently, HSBC parted company with three US DCM MDs, </news/hsbc-fired-more-bankers-before-bonuses-it-s-harsh-and-inhumane>two of whom had been there for nearly two decades each, a week before bonuses were announced. Jerome Pellet, who spent 27 years at the bank and was head of corporate and public sector DCM in France also announced his surprise resignation today after bonuses were announced (but before they were paid). HSBC has also recruited: Jake Hartman, for example, joined as head of FIG DCM from Barclays in August.
HSBC's US DCM business is run by Patrice Altongy. Altongy arrived from Citi in 2021 <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2021-06-21/hsbc-revamps-u-s-investment-bank-as-four-senior-executives-exit>when HSBC overhauled its business and ejected incumbents like Robert Gelnaw, who previously ran DCM and had been there for nearly 20 years.
Altongy <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2021-06-21/hsbc-revamps-u-s-investment-bank-as-four-senior-executives-exit>began her career in M&A at Citi before moving into capital markets. She hasn't always been popular with HSBC's longstanding DCM bankers in New York, but this could change now that they're paid so finely.
HSBC's annual report this week revealed </news/hsbc-is-paying-senior-bankers-who-stay-1-4m-and-senior-bankers-who-go-500k> that it paid 511 material risk takers in investment bank globally an average of $1.4m in 2025, including salaries and bonuses.

item.title: Citi's top inflation trader is joining Brevan Howard; Goldman's is a man of mystery
item.description:
Bonuses are paid and inflation traders are on the move. Citi's top man has gone. So has Goldman's. Only one has a known destination.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
International Financing Review <https://www.ifre.com/people-and-markets/2390184/moves-star-inflation-trader-cypel-leaving-citigroup>reported today that Douglas Cypel, Citi's star inflation trader in Paris, has left Citi. Cypel, who was an MD and Citi's head of G3 inflation trading was said to be joining an unnamed hedge fund. </news/how-to-get-a-hedge-fund-job> We can reveal that this hedge fund is Brevan Howard. Brevan Howard is not commenting.
While Cypel relaxes on gardening leave before his new role, Goldman Sachs' own star inflation trader remains AWOL. As we reported this week, </news/nikhil-choraria>Nikhil Choraria, head of European interest-rate products, and a partner at Goldman Sachs, also resigned.
The market has since been deep in speculation as to Choraria's intentions. Rumour has it that Choraria is joining a major multistrategy hedge fund. Right now, however, we understand that neither Citadel, nor Millennium, nor Balyasny, nor Schonfeld are hiring him. Nor is Choraria understood to be joining Citadel Securities.
42-year-old Choraria was one of Goldman's top earning traders. He joined the firm in 2004 and made a particularly large amount of money in 2021 when Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/features/2022-02-21/how-wall-street-pros-from-goldman-to-jpmorgan-are-winning-the-inflation-trade> reported that Goldman's inflation desk generated $450m in revenues.
Given that Choraria is 42 and spend 22 years at Goldman, he will have benefited from the firm's rule of 60, which states that when tenure and age add up to more than 60 people can walk away and keep all their unvested stock. </news/2023/05/goldman-sachs-layoffs>

item.title: Citadel Securities' is making its ex-Goldman Sachs macro VP famous
item.description:
Frank Flight has flown around. After seven years at Goldman Sachs </news/how-to-get-a-job-goldman-sachs>, he spent 16 months as a portfolio manager </news/2023/09/portfolio-manager-job-hedge-fund>at hedge fund Brevan Howard and then 15 months at hedge fund Soros Fund Management before joining Citadel Securities </news/citadel-securities-keeps-ingesting-europe-s-top-rates-traders> as a strategist in October last year.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
At Citadel Securities, Flight works with Nohshad Shah, his ex-boss at Goldman Sachs and Goldman's former co-head of EMEA interest rates sales. Shah himself has long been something of a star in the markets. At Goldman Sachs he produced a popular weekly note and podcast "Some Macro Thoughts" (now deleted from Goldman's site <https://www.goldmansachs.com/what-we-do/FICC-and-equities/content/pages/one-liner-goldilocks-and-the-3-bears.html>) and at Citadel Securities he is busy doing the same thing <https://www.citadelsecurities.com/news-and-insights/series/macro-thoughts/> as the EMEA head of fixed income.
However, it is Flight, Shah's loyal lieutenant and former VP who has written this week's most noteworthy note. Flight has penned a viral retort to the Citrini Research </news/alap-shah-citrini-research>Substack suggesting that AI will displace high earning white collar workers, who will then be unable to pay their debts and mortgages, creating the mother of all financial crises.
This will not come to pass, says Flight. Mostly because we're nowhere near the level of "compute intensity" that would enable this to happen but also because if we do start nearing this level, the marginal cost of "compute" will rise. In doing so, it may well rise beyond the marginal cost of human labour and make substituting computers for humans unviable. Beyond that, Flight also observes that AI has a positive effect on productivity which will increase purchasing power and consumption and that, in any case, governments wouldn't allow the wholesale displacement of people by machines in a democratic society. Agricultural workers replaced by threshing machines in the 18th century might disagree, but their extinction preceded universal suffrage.
Citadel Securities has been building up its rates business. It hired Andrew Buonauro </news/citadel-securities-is-growing-its-rates-business-with-a-squarepoint-capital-trading-head> from Squarepoint in February and Bapstiste Duboc from BNP Paribas this month.

item.title: Asset manager paying nearly $400k per head hikes compensation amdist AI rollout
item.description:
A new wave of agentic AI </news/agentic-ai-generative-use-cases-finance>systems like OpenClaw and Claude Cowork are the hot topic in financial services technology, but hedge funds </news/how-to-get-a-hedge-fund-job> are ahead of the game. Publicly traded fund group Man Group has been developing its AI </news/how-to-get-a-banking-ai-job>tools for years, coinciding with a steady pruning of its headcount.
Follow eFinancialCareers on Google News here. <https://news.google.com/publications/CAAqMggKIixDQklTR3dnTWFoY0tGV1ZtYVc1aGJtTnBZV3hqWVhKbFpYSnpMbU52YlNnQVAB?ceid=GB:en&oc=3>
In its 2025 annual report released today, Man Group revealed that it had reduced headcount by 3%. This was offset by its acquisition of private credit </news/private-credit> firm Bardin Hill, which had 48 people; excluding those people, headcount fell by 9% from 1,777 to 1,672. It's the second consecutive year of headcount reductions at Man Group after headcount fell a mere 1% in 2024 from a high of 1,790 employees in 2023.
In that time, Man Group has been building out its AI capabilities. It signed a major deal with Claude developers Anthropic earlier this month <https://www.man.com/man-group-anthropic-partnership>, but the fund's Q4 presentation revealed that it had developed 100+ AI plugins prior to the deal, which will likely be operable with Claude Cowork. Speaking in Man Group's investor call, CEO Robyn Grew said there are no specific milestones for the partnership, but said the fund is looking "at the front end and the research capability that we can look at and develop more," as well as other opportunities across the organization.
Man was an early AI adopter, and had developed its own proprietary chatbot, ManGPT, in 2024. Speaking at Quant Strats' London conference in 2024 </news/hedge-fund-says-it-s-not-easy-being-a-prompt-engineer>, then-head of AI Tim Mace said the technology was not "entirely delivering on the hype" but showed promise. Times seem to have changed.
Average compensation spending increased as headcount fell. Fixed and variable compensation (salaries and bonuses) for Man Group staff in 2025 were collectively $392.7k per head, up from $385k in 2024. Bonuses were slightly down, but salaries were up 6.9%, over $10k, on average. The fund said in its 2024 annual report that "overall salaries [were] budgeted to increase by an average of 3.8%" in 2025; it appears to have far exceeded that goal.
Man Group's assets under management climbed 35% last year despite its slumping headcount. The fund's star performer was its multistrategy </news/2023/10/citadel-millennium-hedge-funds> unit, Man 1783, which was up 14% for the year <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-02-26/man-group-assets-hit-record-as-clients-pile-into-long-only-funds>, while its AHL Alpha fund gained 5.5%.

item.title: Ex-Goldman Sachs & Citi trader appears at Morgan Stanley after immense paternity break
item.description:
If you're a male trader concerned about taking time off to spend with your growing family, then do not fear. Taking a lot of time off for paternity leave has become a thing, and it need not damage your career.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
The latest to prove this point is Jamie Peterson who took two years and four months out of the market for what he describes as a "full-time parenting" career break. He is now back, working on the emerging markets desk at Morgan Stanley in New York.
Prior to his long absence, Peterson was a senior member of Citi's emerging markets credit team for two years and an emerging markets credit trader at Goldman Sachs for eight. He didn't respond to a request to comment for this article but former colleagues noted previously that Peterson was a significant contributor to desk pnl while at Goldman Sachs.
He is not the only senior Citi trader to take time out. Akshay Singal, </news/citi-lost-another-top-rates-trader-this-time-to-paternity-leave> Citi's global head of short term interest rate trading (STIRT) took six months paternity leave between June 2025 and January 2026, and is presumably back in his seat.
Long periods of paternity leave are not without risk, though. Jonathan Reeves </news/paternity-leave-investment-banking>, a former compliance VP at Goldman, took six months' leave in 2022 and was terminated five weeks after his return. Man Group </news/hedge-fund-traders-caution-against-paternity-leave-after-trader-dismissed> was rumoured to have removed a trader after a long period of paternity leave last year.
Peterson presumably has now has a toddler instead of a new born and may or may not be well-rested after two and a half years of nappy changes. Writing on LinkedIn, he says his time with his son was a "privilege" but that he is excited to be back.

item.title: Morning Coffee: Most eccentric macro hedge fund manager shows polymaths still matter. Citi copied the Goldman Sachs AI banking team
item.description:
Hedge funds with fancy Greek or Latin names sometimes draw good natured cynicism from the sell side traders and analysts who serve them. Naming your fund after a word meaning “wise” or “agile” or otherwise “good at investing” is in some ways a safe option, but it is often regarded as slightly pretentious, conveying the sense that you regard all this money making stuff as a bit vulgar compared to your true passions in ancient philosophy. Which is a tough look to carry off, if everyone remembers you as a smallcap equities salesperson who used to drink beer out of a shoe.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Tekmerion Capital <https://www.businessinsider.com/zachary-squire-tekmerion-capital-brevan-howard-spinout-hedge-fund-2026-2>, however (a global systematic macro fund </news/2021/05/how-to-get-a-job-in-a-global-macro-fund>with $1bn under management) seems like it might be the real deal. Its co-founder, Zachary Squire, wrote an undergraduate thesis on Cicero which he is turning into a book. And he is nobody’s pseudo-intellectual; although majoring in classics, he also studied quantum cascade lasers <https://www.princeton.edu/news/2008/05/27/grounded-classics-valedictorian-squire-excels-many-fields> at Princeton University, along with trade policy, legal anthropology and Italian opera.
Presumably because of these polymathic interests, he received an unsolicited phone call asking him to apply to DE Shaw and delay his postgraduate studies in classics. This led him to end up at Bridgewater <https://www.institutionalinvestor.com/article/2bsx8ylxkok3og63qk5q8/corner-office/the-origin-story-of-tekmerion-the-hedge-fund-that-ray-dalios-bridgewater-says-stole-its-trade-secrets>, where he apparently excelled on the famous “dot rankings” and met Larry Mincone, Tekmerion’s other co-founder. (Go on then, it’s apparently “irrefutable proof” in Greek philosophy, but literally means “a boundary stone”).
Things then took something of a turn. Mincone and Squire both left Bridgewater for other jobs but when (along with Squire’s old friend, Reed Morrissey) they got back together to set up Tekmerion, Bridgewater got cross that some of the slides in their pitch deck <https://www.businessinsider.com/tekmerion-capital-pitchdeck-ray-dalio-bridgewater-lawsuit-2020-7> had a sort of Ray Dalio-esque feel to them. The resulting arbitration judgement <https://iapps.courts.state.ny.us/nyscef/ViewDocument?docIndex=h3v3OHqpZPkLrvYrPCsinw==> went extremely badly for Bridgewater, with various executives being described as “evasive” and having “manufactured false evidence”. Mincone and Squire won the case in 2020 and got their legal costs awarded, but the team broke up shortly afterward, with Mincone going to another fund and Squire and Morrissey generating a decent track record before taking Tekmerion to Brevan Howard in 2024, from whence it is now emerging.
To some extent, this seems to show that if you can handle Ancient Greek and quantum physics, macro investing is a easy by comparison. (For any polymathic readers who fancy a try, the arbitration judgement suggests that Squire started to build his systematic approach by reading a book called “Expected Returns: An Investor’s Guide to Harvesting Market Rewards <https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/book/10.1002/9781118467190>”). Of course, if you were really as smart as Zachary Squire, people would probably have recognised it by now. But in a world of increasing specialisation and dullness, it’s good to know that there are still old-fashioned general purpose geniuses out there.
Elsewhere, Citigroup seems to be the latest bank to join the trend of setting up a specialised AI Infrastructure <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-02-25/citigroup-assembles-banking-team-focused-on-ai-infrastructure?embedded-checkout=true> group in its investment banking division. As with the Goldman Sachs “Global Technology Infrastructure </news/goldman-sachs-tmt-bankers-restructuring>” team, this reflects the underlying economics of the clients. There are some “tech” companies who write software and algorithms, some “tech” companies that deliver burritos by bicycle, and some “tech” companies that build huge sheds in the middle of nowhere which need massive amounts of electricity and cooling water. The different types of tech clients have very different financing needs, and the “sheds and utilities” tech team will be a lot more adjacent to the private credit markets than to Silicon Valley venture capital.
In the current environment, that might be more glamorous rather than less. Many of the smartest investors and bankers <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-02-25/ubs-global-wealth-cio-says-ditch-coders-for-builders-as-ai-booms> are looking toward heavy industrial assets, power and real estate as the hottest new sectors, while software companies are having a hard time convincing anyone that they have a future in a world dominated by vibe-coding. And the new Citi team is being led by senior rainmakers like Ric Spencer and Ashish Agrawal, with a mandate to “break silos and evaluate all pockets of capital available”. So for ambitious young bankers who want to get a wide range of experience in a team that’s likely to be paying well, it’s a good direction to face in. Just don’t expect the sushi or vegan dining options to be fantastic on all your client field trips.
Meanwhile...
James van Geelen, the author of the Citrini Research blog post that was blamed for a mini crash in the stock market, is a little bit awestruck to discover that he has such powers. (Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-02-24/citrini-founder-shocked-his-ai-prediction-spurred-stocks-selloff?srnd=phx-markets&sref=DK3y4h9m>)
“So you feel uncomfortable doing the job you were hired to do?” It’s more than a decade ago, and they’ve already been fined for it, but the story of Deutsche Bank’s involvement with Jeffery Epstein, and the way that employee concerns were brushed off, still makes pretty painful reading. (FT <https://www.ft.com/content/b1d9e8c4-1874-4c82-941d-9a2e1512bc4c>)
Megan Hogan, Goldman Sachs’ global co-head of talent (and until last year, chief diversity officer) has confirmed that she will be going to Morgan Stanley. (Business Insider <https://www.businessinsider.com/goldman-sachs-diversity-exec-megan-hogan-morgan-stanley-dei-2026-2>)
In the early days of DOGE, a lot of people expected that Elon Musk would be able to make large cuts in government spending. A tax economist who understood that most of the budget is fixed because of pensions and debt interest disagreed. With the agreement of an understanding spouse, he spent his entire life savings buying prediction market contracts from people who didn’t understand the facts quite so well, making a 37% return and a six figure profit. (WSJ <https://www.wsj.com/finance/investing/the-tax-nerd-who-bet-his-life-savings-against-doge-6b59eda2?st=graguy&reflink=desktopwebshare_permalink>)
George Saravelos of Deutsche Bank is still writing FX research despite having been scolded by the US Treasury Secretary. And although Deutsche’s top management were diplomatic about it in the first instance, they have confirmed that research independence is “absolutely sacrosanct”. (Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-02-25/deutsche-s-saravelos-says-he-ll-keep-writing-after-us-criticism?taid=699ed05b5922640001f41f02&utm_campaign=trueanthem&utm_content=business&utm_medium=social&utm_source=twitter>)
It’s got harder and harder for the writers of “Industry” to continue to top each successive season’s antics, so it’s probably for the best that the next season will see Harper, Eric, Yasmin and the gang go out on a high. (Hollywood Reporter <https://www.hollywoodreporter.com/tv/tv-news/industry-renewed-season-5-hbo-final-season-1236514548/>)

item.title: Pay for top performing US investment bankers in 2026: $180k-$2.5m
item.description:
It's 2026 and time to consider whether you were paid fairly for your efforts last year. Most US banks have already announced and paid bonuses, and the disparities are large.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
US recruitment firm Harrison Rush <https://harrisonrush.com/> has got some figures. Shown in the table below, they reflect the fact that even top performers are not paid equally at similar firms.
Harrison Rush says total compensation for top ranked bankers starts at $180k (salary plus bonus) for first year analysts </news/banks-weird-hierarchies-analysts-associates-vps-mds> and extends to $2.5m+ for managing directors in elite boutiques. </news/boutique-investment-banks>
Salaries and bonuses are not broken out for all levels. However, Harrison Rush said previously that bulge bracket managing directors (MDs) receive salaries of between $300k-$450k and that MDs in elite boutiques receive salaries of between $300k and $500k. The rest of their pay is bonus.
Tom Ragland, CEO of Harrison Rush, says the biggest bonus increases went to technology bankers this year. Rival recruiter Meridith Dennes said previously that some top vice presidents (VPs) earned $740k </news/vp-pay-banking> for last year. Ragland has gone $10k higher.

item.title: Standard Chartered’s bankers are hitting new records for pay
item.description:
After the American and European Q4 reporting seasons, it’s reporting season for a more niche type of bank: UK-based banks with their primary operations in Asia. It was HSBC’s turn today </news/hsbc-is-paying-senior-bankers-who-stay-1-4m-and-senior-bankers-who-go-500k>, but Standard Chartered’s turn yesterday <https://www.sc.com/en/investors/financial-results/>.
Click here to join the bubble by eFinancialCareers, our new anonymous community. <https://bubble.efinancialcareers.com/login?_gl=1*ut8n6u*_gcl_au*MTg4MDY2NTExNi4xNzYxNTU3NDIz*_ga*ODczNzI2NTA0LjE3NTM2OTk2ODI.*_ga_NZFQV4MXPM*czE3NjM2NDM0MDYkbzI1NiRnMSR0MTc2MzY0MzQzOSRqMjckbDAkaDMyMjc4OTUzMw..>✍️
Generally, the bank did well. It managed to deliver a year early on its 3-year plan, and it bumped up its bonus pool by 10% to $1.9bn. Kindly, it even budgeted a 2.6% increase in its salaries globally, weighted particularly towards more junior staff.
The real winners, however, are the bank’s highest earners. As well as Q4 of 2025 results, Standard Chartered also published its full-year Basel Pillar 3 report. And that report detailed how the bank paid its “investment banking” material risk takers.
MRTs are defined as people whose “professional activities have a material impact on the risk profile of the firm,” and automatically includes all people who earn over £660k. The “investment banking” function, according to the Basel framework, also appears to include sales & trading </news/how-do-you-get-a-job-in-sales-and-trading-in-a-bank> professionals.
Standard Chartered’s record 385 investment banking MRTs earned an average of $1.48m each for their work in 2025, which was a 9% increase on the $1.36m they earned on average in 2024. The biggest increase came from variable compensation (bonuses </news/banker-bonuses>) paid – from an average of $765k in 2024 to an average of $870k in 2025, an increase of 14% between the two years.
Interestingly, however, Standard Chartered “only” paid 282 over €1m ($1.18m) in 2025, which was a slight decline from the 293 that earned over that sum in 2024. However, the bank paid one person over €16m ($19m), which it hasn’t done since before at least 2019, if ever.

item.title: Ex-Goldman Sachs analyst seeks exceptional people for atypical opportunities
item.description:
If you've tried investment banking </news/what-is-an-investment-bank> and it wasn't for you, there is an alternative. If you've tried private equity </news/how-to-get-a-job-in-private-equity> and it wasn't for you, it's not the end of the line. If you've tried hedge funds </news/how-to-get-a-hedge-fund-job> and still want stimulation, there's another option. You could choose the fintech firm whose founder tried everything and refused to compromise.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Aged 31, Alexis Augier </news/alexis-augier-vega> has been an investment banking analyst at Goldman Sachs, an analyst and associate at KKR, and an investment professional at Elliott Investment Management. Augier left Elliott in 2022 and founded Vega, a platform that provides infrastructure for private markets. Since then, hundreds of people have applied to work with him at Vega. Few have been accepted.
"We hire for exceptional attributes, not experience," explains Augier. Successful candidates are those for whom "being good" has never been enough. They are a species who, "get restless without a challenge and need to feel passionate about everything they do."
If this is you, you can demonstrate your passion by applying for Vega's new "product and growth program" which Augier says will turn "elite generalists into product & business builders." These generalists might come from banking, consulting or "other analytically demanding fields." They will rotate between product and growth roles until they find an area of mutual fit and passion, and will be equipped with "the tools, exposure, and ownership to build and scale products from the ground up, while being exposed to the more commercial and strategic side of the business."
It's not clear what the salary is for these roles, but the upside is described as "uncapped." This might be why the last time that Augier invited applications for generalist vacancies he received 500+ applications in two days. That's a lot considering he currently has two vacancies for the new product and business program.
Augier's recent hires include Mattia Mancino, a former associate in UBS's investment banking team, who's now a product manager, and Sara Saloo, a former BCG consultant who's now head of strategy and growth.
Both roles would usually require years of tenure, says Augier. “But we are not building a typical startup. We are building the kind of company where the level of ambition, excellence, and calibre of the team make it a credible career alternative to top investment firms. That is the bar we hold ourselves to.”

item.title: HSBC has both empowered its people with AI and hired 1,800 technologists
item.description:
HSBC is powering-up. It is paying more </news/hsbc-is-paying-senior-bankers-who-stay-1-4m-and-senior-bankers-who-go-500k>. It is empowering its people. It is firing </news/hsbc-s-pre-bonus-equities-job-cuts>. And it is hiring.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Today's full year and fourth quarter results documents <https://www.hsbc.com/investors/results-and-announcements/all-reporting/group?page=1&take=20&reporting-type=annual&years=2025> from HSBC underscore the smorgasbord of things occurring at the bank. After ruthlessly pivoting </news/hscb-closure-europe-us-job-cuts> away from M&A and ECM outside of Asia and the Middle East and simplifying its businesses, HSBC cut 4,400 jobs and $600m in costs last year. It was only planning to cut $300m in costs, so the target was exceeded.
While this was occurring, HSBC stocked up on technology staff. The bank said today that it "invested" in 1,800 technology full time employees last year. Its technologists have a remit to "empower colleagues" with AI; around 85% of colleagues have been enabled with a Gen AI productivity tool.
HSBC's technology team is led by Stuart Riley, who joined from Citi in 2023. </news/2023/11/stuart-riley-hsbc>Riley, who was well liked at Citi, is reportedly busy simplifying HSBC's IT estate. <https://businesschief.com/news/hsbc-cio-stuart-riley-on-leading-with-data-and-ai>
While HSBC accumulates technologists to work on simplification and AI empowerment, other banks have been slowing the pace of IT hiring. JPMorgan, </news/jpmorgan-banking-jobs-market> for example, said this week it only increased technology headcount by 1% last year.
HSBC's strategy appears popular internally. 78% of its people employees declared themselves engaged in its employee engagement index, down only two percentage points on last year.
It may help that median pay at the bank is up 47% since 2019, as per the chart below from the 2025 annual report.
Pay at HSBC
Source: HSBC

item.title: New trader arrives at Jain Global in Houston
item.description:
Jain Global may have generated subpar returns last year, but it is still hiring.
Click here to join the bubble by eFinancialCareers, our new anonymous community. <https://bubble.efinancialcareers.com/login?_gl=1*ut8n6u*_gcl_au*MTg4MDY2NTExNi4xNzYxNTU3NDIz*_ga*ODczNzI2NTA0LjE3NTM2OTk2ODI.*_ga_NZFQV4MXPM*czE3NjM2NDM0MDYkbzI1NiRnMSR0MTc2MzY0MzQzOSRqMjckbDAkaDMyMjc4OTUzMw..>✍️
Matt Wolcott joined hedge fund Jain Global as a portfolio manager in its commodities team in Houston last month. Wolcott last worked for Citadel, which he left in 2024 after working for Northville Industries, a petroleum storage company, where he was a refined products trader.
Jain declined to comment. Neither its Houston office nor its commodities trading team are new. The commodities team is headed up by </news/citadel-parted-company-with-an-interesting-portfolio-manager-jain-global-hired-him> ex-Macquarie oil trader David Hochberg. It's hired a number of portfolio managers recently, including Jay McCall </news/citadel-parted-company-with-an-interesting-portfolio-manager-jain-global-hired-him>, Bruce Kish </news/blue-crest-is-hiring-commodities-traders-but-people-keep-leaving>, and Yves Mindren <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2025-05-14/millennium-hires-ex-ubs-asia-pacific-equity-derivatives-head>.
Commodities trading was one of Jain’s initial seven strategies </news/jain-global>. While (Bobby) Jain said the fund was designed to easily scale, starting off as a “seven-legged stool” instead of adding new legs as it went along, it’s a bit much – most new hedge funds start with one or two.
As we noted earlier, Jain has posted low returns since formation. In 2025 it was up just 3.7% in its first full year of trading; compared to Citadel’s 10.2% in the same year. Of course, that 3.7% figure is Jain's overall performance, and the commodities team may have done much better. Or worse. Maybe Wolcott was convinced by Jain’s mentoring offer </news/as-jain-global-p-ms-take-headhunter-calls-bobby-jain-explains-why-they-need-patience#:~:text=Bobby%20Jain%20is%20mentoring%20himself>.

item.title: Wells Fargo hired a rates trader from JPMorgan and now he's building a team
item.description:
Nikhil Choraria </news/nikhil-choraria> is not the only man on the move in London now that US banks' bonuses have been paid. Charles Adams, a senior SSA (Sovereigns, Supranationals, and Agencies) trader in London has also made a move. Having done so, he is building a team.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Adams has arrived at Wells Fargo in London, where he is senior lead for European rates securities trading. Adams didn't respond to a request to comment for this article, but we understand that he will be building out the bank's European cash rates trading business. This suggests hiring. The business currently comprises Adams alone.
Adams spent 14 years at JPMorgan and was latterly the desk head for its UK/European Credit and SSA specials trading and financing businesses. His new role at Wells Fargo therefore looks like a promotion.
SSA traders can be popular. Jonas Klink </news/jonas-klink> at UBS, for example, has been the past subject of bidding wars. Bobby Previti, who ran SSA and covered bond trading at Bank of America, is now running a pod a team at Point72. </news/bank-of-america-s-ex-head-of-ssa-trading-is-building-a-pod-at-point72>
Wells Fargo has big plans this year. It's adding managing directors (MDs) in the investment bank and is building out its trading business. Fernando Rivas, head of Wells Fargo's corporate and investment bank told the Financial Times <https://www.ft.com/content/f5ac8471-184e-4425-bd4e-8c2d0197c3d7>in January that the bank is now able to flex its balance sheet and work with hedge fund clients after a seven year long asset cap <https://www.federalreserve.gov/newsevents/pressreleases/enforcement20250603a.htm> was lifted last June.

item.title: Morning Coffee: Hedge funds’ need for juniors is getting intense. European traders have had their best time in a decade
item.description:
If you heard the chief people officer of a multistrategy hedge fund </news/big-multistrategy-hedge-funds>say something like “people are pursuing a career instead of a job <https://www.businessinsider.com/inside-the-hedge-fund-hiring-frenzy-career-ladder-talent-wars-2026-2>”, your first reaction might be to ask if they were feeling all right. Multistrats are more famous for providing not so much structured career development as the opportunity to occupy a desk for as long as you’re making money, and then to clear it out really quickly if you lose any.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
But, if you're a hedge fund, </news/how-to-get-a-hedge-fund-job> that kind of transactional relationship with your employees is expensive. When people are subjecting themselves to the rigours of “pod shop” risk management policies, they want to be paid handsomely for doing so. This is why one of the biggest problems for the multistrategy hedge fund industry has been the war for talent <https://www.hedgeweek.com/multi-manager-hedge-funds-escalate-talent-war-arms-race-with-nine-figure-pay-packages/> and the associated cost inflation, with eight- and even nine-figure packages </news/how-to-get-a-job-at-hedge-fund-millennium>dangled for proven portfolio manager </news/2023/09/portfolio-manager-job-hedge-fund>s (PMs).
Instead of constantly hiring-in new people, Business Insider <https://www.businessinsider.com/inside-the-hedge-fund-hiring-frenzy-career-ladder-talent-wars-2026-2>notes funds like Citadel, </news/2022/10/how-to-get-a-job-at-citadel> Point72 and Balyasny </news/2023/10/balyasny-hedge-fund> are therefore trying to produce their own home-grown talent. Like a sports team, bringing stars through the youth academy is a lot more cost effective than buying them on the open market. Two-thirds of the PMs in Citadel’s fixed income and macro groups were developed in-house, while 60% of Point72’s equity PMs have come through “LaunchPoint”, its program to develop analysts into managers.
It looks like these numbers are calculated in terms of headcount, though, rather than assets under management, risk limits or P&L contribution. Some investors are apparently cynical about people who get into hedge fund investing through a graduate program, rather than by demonstrating their talent somewhere else. As one puts it, “Do you still know how to hunt if you were raised in captivity?”
The pod shops do seem to recognise that there are different management styles needed to deal with juniors stepping up, compared to high-profile external hires who are presumed to be big enough and nasty enough to look after themselves. Ilan Weiss, who manages a technology portfolio for Balyasny, says that coaching junior analysts into running their own trading books is “frankly … not that much different from being a therapist”.
Although one way in which it is quite different from being a therapist is that at Balyasny, when one of your analysts leaves your pod to run their own, you often get “some type of economic interest” in their performance. For managers who are particularly good at recognising and developing talent, this could be a nice little earner.
But you first have to find the raw material. Multi-strategy firms are now using almost as many analytics and quantitative measures to try and select top graduates and match them to the right mentors as they do on managing their own portfolios. There’s an obvious reason for doing so, but it’s an intrinsically difficult problem and luck probably still counts for a lot. As one executive puts it, “half of the battle in becoming a PM is getting in the right situation”.
Elsewhere, the year of “Hotline Bling <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hotline_Bling>”, the first Minions <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Minions_(film)> movie and that dress <https://www.nytimes.com/2015/02/28/business/a-simple-question-about-a-dress-and-the-world-weighs-in.html> was also, for a very long time, the last “good year” for European trading revenues. The Eurocrisis was being resolved, interest rates were low and Deutsche still had an equities business, back in 2015. The record that was set that year got broken in 2022, but it has been absolutely smashed in 2025, with total revenues set to reach €43.9bn.
The causes were partly external and partly internal. Trading businesses always benefit from “good volatility” – not too much but not too little, plenty of changes of direction in an underlying bullish trend. And although it seemed frightening at times, the global climate delivered exactly that.
But more importantly, the secret of making money in trading is usually “not losing it”. As well as a good year for client volumes, 2025 was the first year in a long time when we didn’t see at least one major European bank wasting several years’ worth of profits on some silly mistake or other. Although the traders and risk takers are likely to have been well-paid for their efforts, the risk managers and controllers ought to be looked after too.
Meanwhile …
Verition Fund Management has licensed the Photon trading technology platform, which was regarded as one of the crown jewels of Eisler Capital now that it’s winding up. They have also hired a number of former Eisler employees to work on it. (Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-02-24/verition-signs-deal-for-eisler-s-tech-as-hedge-fund-shutters?embedded-checkout=true>)
Raine Group is betting that the current AI panic will subside and there will be plenty of tech and media IPOs in 2026. They have hired Anthony Kontoleon, most recently at a wealth management firm, but an experienced former equity capital markets banker at Credit Suisse. (WSJ <https://www.wsj.com/finance/raine-group-hires-former-credit-suisse-ipo-veteran-28fd2dc8?mod=mhp>)
The head of product for Claude AI models at Anthropic says that it’s actually “a little bit of an overreach or overreaction” to say that their latest launches are going to undermine everyone else’s business model. (Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-02-24/anthropic-links-ai-agent-with-tools-for-investment-banking-hr>)
Crispin Odey’s lawsuit against the Financial Conduct Authority goes on – in a ruling over what he is or isn’t allowed to sue for, it seems that he will get to make the case that, for some reason, the regulator had an “animus” against him. (FT <https://www.ft.com/content/2c2f956b-c79b-42c4-981c-5dd94ef8e529>)
It might be a bit much to describe the former Milan office of Credit Suisse as “iconic” or “a landmark” – they do have the Duomo and La Scala there after all. But the fact that Goldman Sachs is moving in to the building does most likely indicate a significant expansion of their ambitions in the Italian market. (Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-02-24/goldman-sachs-set-to-move-into-credit-suisse-s-iconic-milan-hq>)
James Fishback became a minor financial celebrity a few years ago after he tried to sue Greenlight Capital for refusing to agree that he had ever had the title “head of macro”. He’s apparently been busy since then, racking up a lot of other colorful episodes on the way to his current campaign to be Governor of Florida. (The Bulwark <https://www.thebulwark.com/p/meet-the-first-groyper-politician-james-fishback?utm_medium=android&triedRedirect=true>)

item.title: HSBC is paying senior bankers who stay $1.4m and senior bankers who go $500k
item.description:
It's HSBC results and bonus day. People there who are still in employment at the bank may find it's been a good year. HSBC has increased its overall bonus pool, and with it the amount it allocates to senior regulated staff known as material risk takers (MRTs).
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
HSBC's total bonus pool for 2025 is up 10% year-on-year to $3.9bn. Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-02-25/hsbc-bonus-pool-rises-to-highest-in-at-least-a-decade> points out that this is its highest level in a decade. The bonus pool paid to HSBC's investment bank MRTs is up by a more modest 6% on 2024.
Individual MRTs in HSBC's investment bank have nonetheless had an 11% increase in their average bonuses after the bank reduced their number by 24 people last year during its ruthless </news/hsbc-s-self-proclaimed-ruthless-ceo-is-not-its-most-feared-person> withdrawal from the equity capital markets and M&A business outside of Asia and the Middle East. It's also been pruning people </news/hsbc-s-pre-bonus-equities-job-cuts>directly before bonuses are announced today.
As per the chart below, average MRT bonuses in the investment bank at HSBC were $819k for 2025, while average MRT salaries there were $584k. Total compensation for HSBC's top investment bank employees therefore averaged $1.4m. This maybe heavily skewed towards a few people though: HSBC reportedly intends to pay zero bonuses </news/hsbc-s-zero-bonuses-risk-upsetting-already-traumatised-bankers> to many people this year, while the highest paid employees at the bank were generously rewarded. Seven earned €6-€7m million and an unnamed individual received €16m-€17m.
HSBC CEO Georges Elhedery reportedly declared that the bank's new compensation structure, focused on an "eat what you kill" </news/hsbc-bonus-worries> approach, will permit the bank “to attract, to hire and to retain talent.”
People let go from HSBC won't be paid for killing anything. Across the bank as a whole, 134 identified staff were let go last year and were given an average of $504k each to see them on their way.
Some senior HSBC people say they would like to receive these parting gifts. "You get a big pay off and a huge tax break. A lot of us have been hoping for it," says one.

item.title: The head of Goldman Sachs European rates desk appears to have gone
item.description:
Nikhil Choraria, head of European interest-rate products, and a partner at Goldman Sachs is understood to have resigned.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Goldman Sachs isn't commenting and we haven't spoken to Choraria himself, but multiple sources say he's leaving. His destination is unclear, but is thought to involve Dubai.
The FCA Register says Choraria joined Goldman as part of the analyst class of 2004. His entire career has been with the firm. He was head of the inflation desk until he was promoted to become the European head of interest products early last year after Nirubhan Pathmanabhan and Yibo Bao, Goldman Sachs' co-head of global rates trading and its head of interest rate options and exotics trading in Europe both retired.

item.title: Top trading engineers are pursuing "generational wealth" at AI firms
item.description:
Making the sort of wealth that will enable your grandchildren to take up crochet is harder than it used to be. But it might still be possible if you join an AI firm soon. With this in mind, recruiters say the procession of engineers leaving trading firms </news/difference-between-electronic-trading-firms> for AI start-ups is really picking up the pace.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
"It's gone crazy," says Joe Long at Edge Executive Search in San Francisco. "Engineers on $2m-$4m at trading firms are joining AI start-ups for equity that could be worth $100m. They know this is their opportunity to make generational wealth."
The enthusiasm is apparently mutual. While trading firm engineers love AI start-ups, AI start-ups also love trading engineers. "AI start-ups don't want people from Google or Meta, they want people from Jump or HRT," proclaims Long. It's because these people really really know how to code, he says. "High frequency trading people have strong tech and research chops. You can be a software engineer doing C++ low latency trading systems or machine learning for algo trading and it's transferable."
Long was the talent acquisition lead for DRW a few years ago, and so is well-placed to lead traders to AI nirvana. By working for AI start-ups, they can make a "strong cash piece" of $300k, says Long. But it's the "heavy" equity component that's really the thing.
This isn't all though. Most trading firms impose punitive non-competes when people leave, and by joining AI start-ups instead rival trading firms, departing engineers and quant researchers can benefit from "double dipping." During the non-compete they continue to draw a salary from their previous employer, while also getting paid by the start-up.
While the AI bubble is inflating, it sounds like a great idea. And even if the bubble bursts, Long says the engineers and quants </news/what-is-a-quant-researcher-hedge-fund> can always go back to trading firms.
The concept of sitting out your non-compete </news/g-research-non-competes> at a firm where it doesn't have teeth is nothing new. In the past, quants and technologists joined Meta and Microsoft to double dip. Now it's Open AI, </news/openai-culture> Anthropic and AI start-ups. Per Karlström, a top FPGA engineer at Hudson River Trading is among those thought to have made the move of late.
There's a sense of urgency to the process. Wall Street Journal <https://www.wsj.com/tech/ai/why-the-tech-world-thinks-the-american-dream-is-dying-daf793dc?gaa_at=eafs&gaa_n=AWEtsqdm556AM9RW0JCh4qBpB0c1tA-1S8Az7KLKV7QMah9lCRnRBuUekBgwoapfUek%3D&gaa_ts=699df3f9&gaa_sig=uk3trEChqRv0Q3q3u-OsAXDHe6KT7GhojXH-r05YhZngGyLVl29qGKUF2XgCexB8oJj88BApKj3n0z20bjBFEQ%3D%3D>reported in January that Silicon Valley professionals are fretting that this is the last opportunity to make generational wealth before AI turns everyone into manual gig workers </news/alap-shah-citrini-research>. Last year, Long says he placed 25 trading firm technologists into AI firms. This year, he's targeting 50 of them.

item.title: HSBC's pre-bonus equities job cuts are fine news for the survivors
item.description:
HSBC's bonus day is nearly upon us. If you work for the bank and have had a good year, you may expect to be paid. Unfortunately, though, HSBC is continuing with its new and ruthless </news/hsbc-fired-more-bankers-before-bonuses-it-s-harsh-and-inhumane> habit of cutting people before bonuses are actually announced.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
The latest people to feel the blade are senior figures in the bank's equities sales and trading </news/how-do-you-get-a-job-in-sales-and-trading-in-a-bank> business. Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-02-24/two-top-hsbc-traders-are-departing-ahead-of-tough-bonus-season?utm_medium=email&utm_source=author_alert&utm_term=260224&utm_campaign=author_20887312>reports that James Grafton, HSBC's global head of cash equities trading and Steve Jobber, its head of cash equities trading for EMEA, are both leaving. Bonuses are announced tomorrow.
Neither Grafton nor Jobber responded to a request to comment for this article. HSBC said it doesn't comment on individuals, but added: "We’re committed to attracting and retaining talent as part of HSBC’s high-performance culture.”
The tacit implication, then, is that Grafton and Jobber might not have fitted HSBC's "high performance culture." However, insiders at the bank say this wasn't so. Instead, we understand that HSBC's cash equities business had an exceptional year, particularly in Europe, where ambitious targets were exceeded by 10%.
Why, then, were Grafton and Jobber let go? As is often the case, the suspicion is that HSBC might be cutting senior people to make the bonus pool go further. The bank's global cash equities business is run by Ed Duggan, who joined from JPMorgan in 2018. With the expensive figures of Grafton and Jobber gone without bonuses, Duggan will now have more money to distribute between the other members of his team.
This may be great news for HSBC equities loyalists, but Grafton and Jobber's exits also suggest more change for a business that has already plenty of that in recent years. Rob Crane, the former EMEA head of equities left in July 2024. Grafton himself joined from Morgan Stanley in Hong Kong in 2018 and moved to London in 2021. Jobber joined two years later, seemingly hired by Grafton. He was part of a coterie of nine ex-Credit Suisse equities traders <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2023-09-19/hsbc-raids-credit-suisse-to-build-global-equities-business>hired by HSBC in 2023.
Grafton and Jobber's disappearances come after HSBC's Hong Kong equities professionals complained l </news/hsbc-s-hong-kong-equities-professionals-are-feeling-forsaken>ast year that the business seemed to be run almost entirely from London. HSBC cut its equity capital markets business (ECM) in the UK and the US early last year during its surprise pivot to Asia and the Middle East. </news/hscb-closure-europe-us-job-cuts>Its Asian equities professionals had expected a higher profile as a result. However, HSBC has since failed to win a role on key IPOs, including CK Hutchison's $2bn dual listing in London and Hong Kong, <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-01-09/ck-hutchison-said-to-pick-goldman-sachs-ubs-for-hk-london-ipo-of-watson-group> and it's thought the lack of a London ECM business might be to blame.
In the wake of Grafton and Jobber's exits, HSBC's EMEA equities trading business will be closely scrutinised for signs of weakness. Most of the research team were cut last year. </news/hsbc-s-latest-cuts-the-senior-people-stayed> Duggan is thought to be building out the electronic trading platform with the help of people like Fabio Martinelli, who also joined in 2018 from Virtu. However, Jobber - who ran a successful event driven book - is thought to have been a key point of contact for the hedge fund clients that are critical for cash equities businesses to thrive. Unfortunately, he's not there any more.

item.title: Interns in electronic trading are earning over $90k for a few months work
item.description:
Electronic trading </news/high-frequency-trading-hiring-and-pay>is home to some of the most lucrative internships in finance, but how much can you really earn? Thanks to New York labour laws, firms are required to provide a salary for their internships </news/this-is-what-it-takes-to-get-a-64k-jane-street-internship>, revealing that students are earning thousands of pounds every week to work as quant traders </news/quant-jobs-finance>, researchers and engineers.
Follow eFinancialCareers on Google News here. <https://news.google.com/publications/CAAqMggKIixDQklTR3dnTWFoY0tGV1ZtYVc1aGJtTnBZV3hqWVhKbFpYSnpMbU52YlNnQVAB?ceid=GB:en&oc=3>
We've broken down pay based on open listings across various top firms. While most only provide salary, the exceptions are Optiver </news/hft-optiver-uk-pay> and Hudson River Trading, which also include sign-on bonuses in their compensation disclosure. Optiver said that, for PhD <https://www.google.com/url?sa=t&rct=j&q=&esrc=s&source=web&cd=&ved=2ahUKEwiZxfbl0_KSAxV0XEEAHaYCBs0QFnoECBsQAQ&url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.efinancialcareers.com%2Fnews%2Fdo-you-need-a-phd-for-a-quant-job-in-finance&usg=AOvVaw05S9dBtg633HrL3pIH0KV4&opi=89978449> quant research interns, it's paying a total compensation of $80k to $90k over the whole internship; if the internship lasts eight weeks like some of Optiver's other opportunities, that would mean quant research interns earn up to $11.25k per week in total compensation.
The rest of the trading firms </news/difference-between-electronic-trading-firms> (which also pay signing bonuses), only provide salary ranges. The highest salary is on offer at the New York office of London-based trading firm XTX Markets </news/lucrative-electronic-market-maker-xtx-pays-19-9m-per-head-to-its-30-partners>. Its AI research internship pays $35k per month in base salary ($8.75k per week) and lasts 12-14 weeks. This means XTX interns can earn $122.5k in salary alone for the full period.
Jane Street, </news/how-to-get-a-job-at-jane-street> which paid $1.4m on average to its staff in 2024, pays most of its interns the equivalent of a yearly salary of $300k. </news/jane-street-intern-pay>Quant traders, quant researchers, machine learning engineers, FPGA engineers, network engineers and windows engineers all earn that base.
If you can convert your internship, you'll earn much more. IMC Trading, which paid interns up to $5.8k per week, is thought to have offered total compensation packages of $425k </news/a-high-frequency-trading-firm-is-offering-its-interns-425k-to-come-back-as-open-ai-looms> for last year's cohort to return as graduates.
Additionally, Citadel Securities' interns are offered the choice between a $15k housing stipend or premium corporate housing.
Have a confidential story, tip, or comment you’d like to share? item.title: Interns in electronic trading are earning over $90k for a few months work
item.description:
Electronic trading </news/high-frequency-trading-hiring-and-pay>is home to some of the most lucrative internships in finance, but how much can you really earn? Thanks to New York labour laws, firms are required to provide a salary for their internships </news/this-is-what-it-takes-to-get-a-64k-jane-street-internship>, revealing that students are earning thousands of pounds every week to work as quant traders </news/quant-jobs-finance>, researchers and engineers.
Follow eFinancialCareers on Google News here. <https://news.google.com/publications/CAAqMggKIixDQklTR3dnTWFoY0tGV1ZtYVc1aGJtTnBZV3hqWVhKbFpYSnpMbU52YlNnQVAB?ceid=GB:en&oc=3>
We've broken down pay based on open listings across various top firms. While most only provide salary, the exceptions are Optiver </news/hft-optiver-uk-pay> and Hudson River Trading, which also include sign-on bonuses in their compensation disclosure. Optiver said that, for PhD <https://www.google.com/url?sa=t&rct=j&q=&esrc=s&source=web&cd=&ved=2ahUKEwiZxfbl0_KSAxV0XEEAHaYCBs0QFnoECBsQAQ&url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.efinancialcareers.com%2Fnews%2Fdo-you-need-a-phd-for-a-quant-job-in-finance&usg=AOvVaw05S9dBtg633HrL3pIH0KV4&opi=89978449> quant research interns, it's paying a total compensation of $80k to $90k over the whole internship; if the internship lasts eight weeks like some of Optiver's other opportunities, that would mean quant research interns earn up to $11.25k per week in total compensation.
The rest of the trading firms </news/difference-between-electronic-trading-firms> (which also pay signing bonuses), only provide salary ranges. The highest salary is on offer at the New York office of London-based trading firm XTX Markets </news/lucrative-electronic-market-maker-xtx-pays-19-9m-per-head-to-its-30-partners>. Its AI research internship pays $35k per month in base salary ($8.75k per week) and lasts 12-14 weeks. This means XTX interns can earn $122.5k in salary alone for the full period.
Jane Street, </news/how-to-get-a-job-at-jane-street> which paid $1.4m on average to its staff in 2024, pays most of its interns the equivalent of a yearly salary of $300k. </news/jane-street-intern-pay>Quant traders, quant researchers, machine learning engineers, FPGA engineers, network engineers and windows engineers all earn that base.
If you can convert your internship, you'll earn much more. IMC Trading, which paid interns up to $5.8k per week, is thought to have offered total compensation packages of $425k </news/a-high-frequency-trading-firm-is-offering-its-interns-425k-to-come-back-as-open-ai-looms> for last year's cohort to return as graduates.
Additionally, Citadel Securities' interns are offered the choice between a $15k housing stipend or premium corporate housing.

item.title: Hundreds of thousands of people applied for jobs at this fintech. Hardly any got in
item.description:
UK-based </news/top-london-fintechs>payments firm Checkout.com </news/fintech-return-to-office-culture-checkout> is a popular place to work. Last year, it received 208,000 applications for 700 jobs but grew its headcount by just 100 people. It had an acceptance rate of less than 0.4% for its jobs, making it more choosy than Goldman Sachs, which hires 1% of its yearly applicants.
Follow eFinancialCareers on Google News here. <https://news.google.com/publications/CAAqMggKIixDQklTR3dnTWFoY0tGV1ZtYVc1aGJtTnBZV3hqWVhKbFpYSnpMbU52YlNnQVAB?ceid=GB:en&oc=3>
Checkout's CEO, Guillaume Pousaz, released a 2025 annual letter stating that Checkout ended last year with 2,000 staff, up from 1,900 at the end of 2024. This is a far cry from 2020 and 2021, when he said that the firm hired 1,500 people. The pace of hiring may soon increase though; Pousaz said Checkout is eyeing a headcount of “2,500 professionals worldwide” by the end of 2026.
Only 15% of roles at Checkout were filled internally. The fact that 700 jobs lead to headcount growth of just 100 would therefore suggest that many roles weren't filled. A Checkout spokesperson said the 700 figure includes "growth positions, internal promotions that create backfill vacancies, pipeline hiring that spans year-end, and roles that may be posted but not ultimately filled within the calendar year."
Checkout may have decided not to hire certain roles as it adopted AI. Pousaz said that 97% of Checkout staff use the technology daily and that its engineers write “2.7 million lines of AI generated code </news/developers-are-doubling-their-code-output-with-ai-is-that-a-bad-thing> monthly.” Checkout has fully automated some tasks with AI, including handling disputes for rejected transactions, “previously a manual task.”
The hiring is happening away from Checkout’s UK headquarters. Of its 169 currently open jobs, just 72 are in London. Headcount in the UK has decreased in recent years; accounts filed across its UK entities via Companies House show headcount fell from 1,157 in 2023 to 1,066 in 2024. This is down from a high of 1,242 in 2022. UK staff still appear to be the majority at Checkout, but barely.
Checkout’s hiring in the US is more abundant. Pousaz said US hiring was up 80% in 2025 and that it opened new offices in both San Francisco </news/2023/01/jpmorgan-san-francisco-engineering> and Atlanta last year.
Pousaz's letter touted a net promoter score (NPS) in the “top quartile of technology businesses;” those disgruntled by Pousaz’s decision to bring staff into the office </news/checkout-guillaume-pousaz-work-from-home> three days per week back in 2023 </news/2023/08/fintech-work-from-home>seem to have learned to live with it or moved on.

item.title: Citrini Research's apocalyptic Substack came from an ex-Citadel analyst early to AI
item.description:
Two men, neither of whom work for large financial services firms, have spooked the markets. For anyone not yet aware of the Citrini Research Substack post <https://www.citriniresearch.com/p/2028gic> titled "THE 2028 GLOBAL INTELLIGENCE CRISIS," [all caps] it has caused a sensation. James Van Geelen, chief executive of Citrini Research, and Alap Shah, a Florida-based AI entrepreneur and investor have co-written what is described alternately as a "scary bedtime story" <https://www.noahpinion.blog/p/the-citrini-post-is-just-a-scary>and a dystopian view of AI-driven civilisational and financial breakdown.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
The (very) short version is their premise that the US economy has been built on a "giant rent-extraction layer on top of human limitations" in which companies have monetised "things that take time" and where "most people are willing to accept a bad price to avoid more clicks." This layer will no longer apply as AI agents "remove friction." The people doing the well-paid jobs in the rent extraction economy will be displaced. They will no longer be able to repay their debts. They will no longer be able to repay their mortgages. The mother of all financial crises will ensue.
The two charts below summarise the fundamentals of the thesis. Karl Marx would probably approve.
What equips Van Geelen and Shah to make such pronouncements? Geelen, a former medic, has been running Citrini Research since 2023 and says he's achieved "+100% net total return since inception" on the "Cintrindex Thematic Portfolio" (which you must pay to view) <https://www.citriniresearch.com/p/the-citrindex>. Shah himself worked briefly for Viking Global Investors and then for hedge fund Citadel as an analyst for two short years until 2011 before leaving to found an AI-powered financial search and information platform and venture capital firm. These days, he's also CEO of Littlebird, an AI assistant that accompanies you around the internet and automates tasks for you.
Shah might therefore be accused of talking his book, which he's been talking for 15 years already. If Littlebird and similar AI agents really take off, his scenario might come to pass.
For the moment, though, JPMorgan is struggling to achieve coding improvements </news/jpmorgan-banking-jobs-market> of more than 10% from AI, and the technology has only led to annual savings of $150m despite the bank's enormous expense line of $2bn a week.
Littlebird's own employees include hopeful engineers drawn from the likes of Goldman Sachs. Most of its developers are based in India, where Van Geelen and Shah say engineering jobs are at particular risk of the agentic AI apocalypse. Shah's own staff need to hope that their CEO is wrong.
