item.title: Morning Coffee: JPMorgan revealed the reality of the banking jobs market now. The primary rule for analyst sleeping hours
item.description:
If you're wondering why you can't find a new banking job now, there are many things to blame. London vacancies are down nearly 80% </news/london-banking-jobs-fallen> in 10 years. The market is awash with candidates </news/banking-recruitment-s-sorry-story-more-candidates-fewer-jobs>; banks are waiting for existing people to leave rather than hiring new ones. And to the extent that jobs are being added, they are not in the high volume areas of the recent past.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
JPMorgan's mini-investor presentation <https://www.jpmorganchase.com/content/dam/jpmc/jpmorgan-chase-and-co/investor-relations/documents/2026-company-updates/firm-overview.pdf> yesterday confirmed the sorriness of the situation. Last year, JPMorgan said its headcount changed by circa 0%. Its biggest headcount growth area was its revenue supporting and front office </news/front-office-middle-office-back-office-banking> jobs, which were up by 4%. JPMorgan's operations </news/operations-jobs-banking> headcount fell by 4%; its support headcount was down by 2% and its technology headcount - once a boom area for hiring - was up by a whisp of 1%.
JPMorgan didn't break out the number of jobs it has in each of these areas. However, front office jobs are never the most populous. At Deutsche Bank, which publishes its precise headcount numbers each quarter, only 25% of jobs involving the investment bank </news/what-is-an-investment-bank> are in the front office. The rest are support and other jobs, which Deutsche Bank - like JPMorgan - is trying to massage down.
While JPMorgan keeps a tight grip on overall headcount, the Financial Times <https://www.ft.com/content/05527e62-1ba3-486d-a4c7-66dd0ccdccad> notes that it's still spending $2bn a week. What's this going on, apart from expensive new banking managing directors (MDs)? </news/wells-fargo-md-hiring> Partly, technology. Business Insider <https://www.businessinsider.com/jpmorgan-tech-budget-ai-20-billion-jamie-dimon-2026-2>observes that JPMorgan plans to spend $20bn on technology this year, an increase of 10% or $1.9bn on 2025.
Yesterday's JPMorgan presentation also elaborated upon how this technology budget is being spent. The biggest chunk overall - $8.8bn - is going to the corporate and investment bank (CIB). $1.2bn of this year's additional budget is going to "products platforms, features and capabilities" across the bank (including machine learning and AI), which in combination also account for 46% of the total tech spending. Inflation accounts for another $0.8bn of this year's extra $1.9bn.
What happened to all the cost savings from AI? There's little sign of this, yet. Last year, JPMorgan said it achieved a mere $150m of "efficiencies" relating to the use of AI in its technology team. Coding improvements from AI are so far only around 10%. In the meantime, JPMorgan is spending heavily on the technology, and probably won't be hiring extra people unless they're the sort that can bring in new clients to generate the revenues that will enable more investment in technology but not technologists.
Separately, as the cancelled Centerview court case shakedown continues </news/lloyd-blankfein-goldman-sachs>, embers are still being raked as to what might have been.
The FT <https://www.ft.com/content/5cf20f55-bf7c-40de-8b88-50b01c048ffc> notes that Kathryn Shiber had been questioning the whole dynamic of bank working hours, and the necessity of being available 24/7. “I would really appreciate if we could strategise how we/I can be more efficient earlier in the day,” she'd asked in an email before being fired by Centerview for insisting upon eight hours sleep. Had the trial gone ahead, this might have been fully debated.
Given that the trial didn't go ahead, junior bankers must instead use another email from the court documents to govern their behaviour. Business Insider <https://www.businessinsider.com/junior-bankers-work-hours-shiber-centerview-wall-street-lawsuit-2026-2> notes that while Shiber was an analyst, her associate </news/banks-weird-hierarchies-analysts-associates-vps-mds> Timothy Ernst complained that he shouldn't be up working late on his own [while Shiber was asleep]. Let this be a reminder: when you're the analyst, you can only sleep when everyone senior to you has signed off.
Meanwhile...
JPMorgan says it's a boom quarter for both trading and investment banking. Trading revenues in Q1 could be $10bn for the first time ever. Investment banking fees could rise by the mid-teens. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-02-23/jpmorgan-lifts-net-interest-income-outlook-ahead-of-firm-update?sref=DK3y4h9m>
US banks made $300bn in profits last year. “It’s another year where we have skipped past any credit concerns which to me is remarkable.” (Financial Times) <https://www.ft.com/content/8fc14314-7300-41f1-acb6-bc525bf2157a>
It's a terrible time to work in private equity. Distributions as a percentage of net asset value were 14% last year — the second-lowest level since the depths of the 2008 financial crisis. Private equity firms already sold their best assets and now investors only want to commit money to vehicles that deliver net internal rates of return exceeding 20%. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-02-23/private-equity-s-dry-spell-now-worse-than-2008-crisis-bain-says?cmpid=022326_marketsdaily&utm_medium=email&utm_source=newsletter&utm_term=260223&utm_campaign=marketsdaily>
The software sector accounted for roughly 18 per cent of US private equity deal value in 2025, according to PitchBook data. (Financial Times) <https://www.ft.com/content/c43b31d7-0b57-4d65-88d9-b74fceadfb5c>
But Blackstone is growing in the Middle East where it says the office could expand to become like Singapore, where it has 100 people. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-02-23/blackstone-s-gray-sees-tipping-point-for-deals-in-middle-east?utm_medium=email&utm_source=author_alert&utm_term=260223&utm_campaign=author_18657817>
Working for the CFTC regulator has become a nightmare. Michael Selig, the chairman, ss doing the work of five commissioners and an uncountable number of departed staff. (FT) <https://www.ft.com/content/b5d9920c-25f8-4799-bd32-39f07ae97fee>
The FCA's workload is booming but its keeping headcount stable. (Financial News) <https://www.fnlondon.com/articles/fca-chair-we-wont-hire-more-staff-to-take-on-ballooning-workload-7cf1c022?mod=homepage>
IBM shares plummet after Anthropic said Claude can help modernize Cobol. (Bloomberg) < https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-02-23/ibm-shares-plunge-as-anthropic-touts-cobol-modernization-efforts>
A dystopian vision of the years to come. Beware the human intelligence displacement spiral. 'White-collar workers saw their earnings power (and, rationally, their spending) structurally impaired. Their incomes were the bedrock of the $13 trillion mortgage market - forcing underwriters to reassess whether prime mortgages are still money good.' (Citrini Research) <https://www.citriniresearch.com/p/2028gic>
49 year-old Joshua Easterley of Sixth Street is retiring as president and co-CIO after his father died. My father passed away a little over a year ago — somewhat unexpected and complicated for me.” Easterley has three daughters and wants “to fill any actual or perceived deficit I have with them. I want to start compounding and avoid any ‘drawdowns.’” (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-02-23/sixth-street-co-founder-joshua-easterly-49-to-retire-from-firm?srnd=phx-industries-finance>
BNP Paribas seemed to demand that New York staff attend the office despite the snow. (Litquidity) <https://www.instagram.com/stories/litquidity/3838608282059942922/>
Have a confidential story, tip, or comment you’d like to share? Contact: +44 7537 182250 (SMS, Whatsapp or voicemail). Telegram: @SarahButcher. Signal: sarahbutcher.22 Click here to fill in our anonymous form </about/editor-tips>, or email editortips@efinancialcareers.com.

item.title: The ex-Goldman Sachs bankers and others earning $1.5m on average at Blue Owl
item.description:
Private credit firm </news/private-credit> Blue Owl Capital is not what it was. Shares in the firm have fallen 60% in the past 13 months and it's become the poster child for problems in the private capital industry. “A shadowy private credit firm is suddenly blocking investors from withdrawing their money,” observed US Senator Elizabeth Warren last week. Blue Owl would almost certainly object to that categorisation, but it does seem that the party might be drawing to a close.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
It was fun while it lasted. Blue Owl, which didn't respond to a request to comment for this article, says it employs circa 1,365 people. Last year, it paid them a combined $1.3bn in bonuses plus a further $765m in compensation from "fee related earnings." Some of the fee related earnings were in the form of stock which has since depreciated. Even so, the implication is that the average Blue Owl employee received $957k in salary and bonus, plus $560k in fee related payments. That's $1.5m in total.
High pay explains why Blue Owl's 1,365 people include an array of names with experience at Goldman Sachs and often Credit Suisse before that. Craig Packer, who co-founded Blue Owl's predecessor Owl Rock Capital in 2016, was global head of high yield capital markets at Credit Suisse and then a partner at Goldman Sachs. Jonathan Lamm, Blue Owl's CFO, spent 22 years at Goldman. Logan Nicholson, the president of diversified lending, was at Goldman for 18 years. Chris Crampton, the head of strategic equity, was there for nearly 20. Senior Blue Owl managing director was a former Goldman head of TMT leveraged finance capital markets. Another senior MD, Jon ten Oever, came the Credit Suisse-Goldman Sachs route. The names are legion.
It's a reunion. But there's been a lot more than Prosecco and vol-au-vents.
Blue Owl's perceived problem is its exposure to software companies. As the firm has been used to proudly pointing out, it has a team of 40 "tech-focused investment professionals," led by Erik Bissonnette (ex-Wachovia and BofA), and it has "always liked software." It still does. In the words of Craig Packer last week: "We have a significant team. We think we're one of the largest investors and have the capacity to differentiate between a software business that's going to be well protected in an AI world and one that's going to be more vulnerable."
Not everyone is so certain. Although Blue Owl is adamant that its decision to change the redemption schedule for its $1.6bn OBDC II retail fund from 5% of the fund's value per quarter to episodic redemptions is really and honestly no big deal, investors are not so sure. They might like their cash back now. In an attempt at reassurance, Blue Owl said it already sold some of OBDC II's loan book at 99.8% of the carrying value, but as Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/newsletters/2026-02-20/blue-owl-creates-a-new-private-credit-escape-hatch>observes, some of the buyers were entities affiliated to Blue Owl. By comparison, veteran credit investor Boaz Weinstein is offering to buy shares in OBDC II (also known as Blue Owl Capital Corporation II) for a discount of 35%.
It's presumably a worrying time for Blue Owl's 40 tech-focused investment professionals and the rest. They can at least console themselves with their $957k average salaries and bonuses from last year, though.
Blue Owl's co-chief executives, Doug Ostrover (ex-head of leveraged finance at Credit Suisse) and Marc Lipschultz (ex-KKR), have a little more to lose. Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-02-09/blue-owl-founders-pledged-1-9-billion-stake-before-stock-plunge> reported on February 9th that that the two men juiced their compensation by using a combined $1.8bn of their Blue Owl stock (valued last April) as collateral for loans from financial institutions. That stock has since fallen 39%, or by $702m.

item.title: Goldman Sachs partner reveals what it takes to get a job in its hottest division
item.description:
Goldman Sachs </news/lloyd-blankfein-goldman-sachs> has very high hopes for its asset and wealth management division. It was the hot topic of the bank's last quarterly earnings call; CEO David Solomon </news/kathy-ruemmler-goldman-sachs> said the bank has a "desire to put more feet on the ground to broaden our footprint and our platform." Speaking on a recent podcast <https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=hE0yX58Cafo>, one of the division's partners revealed the kinds of people it's looking for.
Follow eFinancialCareers on Google News here. <https://news.google.com/publications/CAAqMggKIixDQklTR3dnTWFoY0tGV1ZtYVc1aGJtTnBZV3hqWVhKbFpYSnpMbU52YlNnQVAB?ceid=GB:en&oc=3>
Kristin Olsen, head of alternatives for Goldman's wealth business and recent inductee to the bank's management committee, <https://www.goldmansachs.com/pressroom/press-releases/2026/goldman-sachs-announces-leadership-updates> said she lucked into Goldman's wealth division. Olsen initially joined Goldman's investment bank </news/what-is-an-investment-bank> because it was "prestigious" </news/goldman-sachs-md-pay> and had a good analyst training programme </news/how-to-get-a-job-goldman-sachs>. She had "never heard of alternatives" when she was rotated in, but said applicants today have no excuse to be in the same position.
Olsen said there's an "abundance of information" today compared to when she joined Goldman, and said "you just have to be more prepared now." She said that you should exhaust publicly available resources before relying on your network to "let you in under the cover and tell you what actually happens behind the walls of Goldman Sachs." That's not to say you shouldn't use your network, but Olsen said you should always aim to "go into a conversation with somebody already on second base."
Some things never change though; Olsen said to thrive in Goldman's wealth division, you need to be "personable, a good communicator." This is something hedge fund </news/how-to-get-a-hedge-fund-job> managers like Ken Griffin </news/2023/10/citadel-goldman-sachs-114> have frequently cited as the key appeal of hiring from Goldman Sachs. Olsen said this is especially important in wealth where "being able to relate to clients and being able to explain things to them is incredibly important."
Goldman is looking to grow its wealth team with people that fit that bill. In the earnings call, Solomon said the bank's "ultra-high-net-worth franchise is extraordinary." While it grew at a slower rate than Goldman's banking division in the past, Solomon said the tables have now turned and "growth in asset and wealth management is faster."

item.title: Citi's stock bonuses were paid on Friday, but there is often an issue
item.description:
Citi's bankers have got their bonuses </news/citi-salaries-london>. Or have they? Multiple insiders at the US bank say the process of awarding stock bonuses at Citi is almost always complicated.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
The issue relates to the ComputerShare accounts, which are credited with Citi's stock bonuses as they vest. However, sources at the bank say the accounts don't update immediately with the vested stock. It can take days and sometimes even weeks for it to appear and it does so only erratically. Selling the stock isn't immediately possible as a result.
"They tell you how many shares they pay you but you often only receive a small amount at a time," says one Citi managing director. "You know the vested price, but you can't sell it," he adds.
The process is a source of frustration. Although a proportion of Citi bonuses vested in January and then again on February 20th (split across a four year cycle), Citi's stock bonus recipients say it's necessary to repeatedly refresh their accounts to see whether the stock has become available. In some cases, insiders say the stock price has fallen by the time their accounts are finally updated.
The issue doesn't afflict everyone at the bank. We understand that staff who are members of Citi wealth get their shares delivered immediately and can immediately sell them on. Many of those we spoke to said they hadn't been given the option of joining Citi wealth, however. Others claimed that they have to pay commission to sell through ComputerShare and that this isn't the case at rival banks.
"If this happened at another bank there would be outrage," claims one insider.
There are nontheless some advantages to Citi's bonuses this year. In a possible response to changing regulations </news/banking-bonuses-london>, recipients in London say the bank has removed a one year restriction that prevented them from selling the stock. As a result, February 20th this year saw both last year's and this year's bonuses vesting simultaneously.
We understand, too, that some senior people already have their full stock allocation in their ComputerShare accounts; others do not. When the stock hits, Citi bankers and traders are free to go. There are suggestions that bankers in some teams will leave due to disgruntlement with recently hired colleagues.
A spokesperson for Citi said: "Citi is committed to a competitive rewards structure. There has been no change to how Citi delivers shares or the options we provide colleagues for share delivery."

item.title: Electronic trader Virtu Financial now seemingly pays above $500k per head
item.description:
Virtu Financial lacks a lot of the mystique of its rivals in electronic trading </news/high-frequency-trading-hiring-and-pay> given that it's a publicly traded company, but that has it's benefits. While reports on pay at firms like Jane Street </news/this-is-what-it-takes-to-get-a-64k-jane-street-internship> are unofficial, Virtu files a 10-K <https://ir.virtu.com/node/12471/html>annual report with the SEC, breaking down headcount and compensation expense.
The most recent 10k report suggests Virtu is hiring, and becoming an increasingly lucrative place to work.
Follow eFinancialCareers on Google News here. <https://news.google.com/publications/CAAqMggKIixDQklTR3dnTWFoY0tGV1ZtYVc1aGJtTnBZV3hqWVhKbFpYSnpMbU52YlNnQVAB?ceid=GB:en&oc=3>
Virtu's filing showed that, as of February 13th, it had 1,027 employees, up from 969 that time last year. It had a slight bias towards hiring in Asia, which represented 12% of total headcount, a 1 percentage point increase from last year.
Virtu reported a compensation expense (including payroll taxes) of $528m for the year ending December 31st 2025. It's not clear how many employees Virtu had at that point but, if we use the February headcount figure as an estimate, the firm looks to be paying roughly $514k per head. This is up from $449k in 2024 (last year's report has a similar issue). This looks slightly lacklustre compared to industry leaders Jane Street </news/how-to-get-a-job-at-jane-street>, which paid staff $1.4m per head </news/jane-street-s-1-4bn-pay-is-less-communist-than-2005-goldman-sachs> in 2024 and seemingly bumped pay up in 2025.
Virtu isn't finished hiring. The report stated that the firm plans to "continue making, significant investments… includ[ing] increases in headcount and other employee-related expenditures." It currently has 31 jobs, including 17 in the US, nine in Europe (primarily Dublin </news/virtu-financial-high-frequency-trading-pay>) and five across Hong Kong and Singapore. </news/as-jane-street-and-other-prop-trading-firms-expand-in-asia-these-are-the-new-faces-and-new-teams>

item.title: Citadel Securities made another major equities hire in Hong Kong
item.description:
Citadel Securities is not just about high speed equities market making. As a table circulating </news/difference-between-electronic-trading-firms>earlier this year made clear, it does medium frequency trades </news/high-frequency-trading-hiring-and-pay>too. These include structured product trades, which the firm is pushing into in Asia.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Citadel Securities' latest recruit for this business is Clement Florentin, the former head of equity EMEA structuring and head of QIS structuring for APAC at Nomura. We reported </news/now-nomura-s-global-head-of-rates-and-fx-trading-resigned>Florentin's exit last month.
Florentin is joining Citadel Securities as head of structured solutions based in Hong Kong. He will arrive there in May.
He's Citadel Securities' latest major structured products hire in Asia. The electronic trading firm has also recruited Richard Smerin, Morgan Stanley's APAC head of equity derivatives as global head of structured products. Smerin resigned earlier this month.
Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-02-10/citadel-securities-hires-smerin-as-structured-products-head>reported a few weeks ago that Citadel Securities has doubled its Asian headcount in the past five years. Mayank Narang, Millennium's former head of Asian equities, is also joining the Asian COO team.

item.title: Another HSBC investment bank escapee joins Deutsche Bank in London
item.description:
Last summer, as HSBC was winding down its M&A and equity capital markets (ECM) businesses </news/hscb-closure-europe-us-job-cuts> in Europe and the US, swathes of managing directors (MDs) began looking for new jobs in a difficult market with mixed success. </news/banking-pay-falling> One of its senior bankers focused on UK coverage used the cuts as an opportunity for a career break; he just resurfaced at Deutsche Bank.
Follow eFinancialCareers on Google News here. <https://news.google.com/publications/CAAqMggKIixDQklTR3dnTWFoY0tGV1ZtYVc1aGJtTnBZV3hqWVhKbFpYSnpMbU52YlNnQVAB?ceid=GB:en&oc=3>
Christopher Fincken announced earlier today that he's joining Deutsche Bank as an MD in its UK investment banking coverage team. He spent just under four years as a HSBC MD UK coverage team, joining from Nomura. He notably worked on the completion of Fortress' £354m acquisition of Loungers </news/hsbc-bankers-say-there-are-3-lists-they-want-to-know-which-one-they-re-on#:~:text=Christopher%20Fincken%2C%20a%20UK%20M%26A%20MD%2C%20announced%20yesterday%20that%20his%20team%20had%20completed%20the%20acquisition%20of%20%C2%A3354m%20of%20Loungers%20plc%20by%20Fortress.> after the closure announcement, but left the bank in July.
In his announcement post on LinkedIn, Fincken said he'd taken time off to spend with family, including the birth of his third child. While he was away, Deutsche Bank made multiple hires from HSBC. In September, it hired Lisa McGeough <https://www.reuters.com/business/finance/deutsche-bank-hires-lisa-mcgeough-americas-head-hsbc-2025-09-02/> as its US CEO; she held a similar role at HSBC and was head of banking in the region. In October </news/deutsche-bank-rehired-one-of-the-citi-s-top-bankers-again>, its UK-based vice chairman of investment banking, Anthony Parsons joined Deutsche Bank as executive chairman of banking and capital markets. Dan Bailey, HSBC's global head of technology media and telecom (TMT) also left for Deutsche Bank in June. </news/it-was-deutsche-bank-that-hired-hsbc-s-big-tech-banker>
Could we see more HSBC bankers pop up at major rivals this year? Headhunters told us last year </news/hsbc-m-ds-launch-their-new-career-journeys-at-the-most-terrible-time> that it was a particularly tough period for hiring, but as markets pick up, there may be more optimism in 2026. It's not just M&A and ECM bankers on the market, of course; HSBC made cuts to its US debt capital markets (DCM) team </news/hsbc-fired-more-bankers-before-bonuses-it-s-harsh-and-inhumane> just last week.

item.title: Nomura is thought to be hiring ANZ's senior escapees in Singapore
item.description:
Things are afoot in Singapore. As we reported last month <https://shorturl.at/Z8M1H>, there have been more senior departures from ANZ. And as we reported this month, there have also been senior exits from Nomura. <https://shorturl.at/Z8M1H>
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Now that Chinese New Year is over, there are suggestions that the two things may be linked.
Neither Nomura nor the individuals concerned are commenting, but the suggestions are strong that Anshu Sidher, the former global head of markets at ANZ is joining Nomura. Herr-Ling Yeo, ANZ's former head of Global FX and commodities, is thought to be going too.
Nomura has a gap that Yeo could fill. Hoe Lon Leng, its head of FX and flow rates trading resigned two weeks ago. It's not clear where he's going next.
Some of ANZ's people have not chosen Nomura, though. Ed Gibson, a G4 rates trader at the bank has just arrived as head of USD rates for the Asia Pacific region at Wells Fargo.
Nomura's rates business underperformed last year. It's also lost various people in London, including Kilian Frensch </news/kilian-frensch>, Kunal Parekh </news/another-trader-left-nomura-s-leaky-rates-team-before-christmas>, Gian Ameri William Johnson, Quinn Borley and Ruchelle Beltia. More exits are feared after the Japanese bank announces bonuses. If Yeo and Sidher arrive, they could restock the business with other macro escapees from ANZ.

item.title: Morning Coffee: Goldman Sachs' ex-CEO smoked weed and was too dominant in meetings. How to make $1m from 3 months' work as a banking analyst
item.description:
Lloyd Blankfein, the ex-CEO of Goldman Sachs </news/how-to-get-a-job-goldman-sachs>, has written a book. Speaking to the Wall Street Journal <https://www.wsj.com/finance/investing/lloyd-blankfein-goldman-sachs-memoir-ceo-b36c0542?mod=hp_lead_pos8>, he says he's done so partly to explain himself to his children, who noticed his absence during their formative years. It might also be an opportunity to apologise to his wife, whom he hung up upon during personal calls in favour of clients. More generally, it's a paean to the awkward youth who becomes the billionaire.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Blankfein would not have ended up as Goldman Sachs CEO were it not for the influence of some 'religious Christians' he met at Harvard University. Blankfein - who grew up in Brookyln was living alone in his second year at Harvard, neglecting his studies and smoking 'weed and hash.' An extract from his book published in Vanity Fair <https://www.vanityfair.com/culture/story/goldman-sachs-lloyd-blankfein>, details how the Christians, who were serious about their studies, invited him into their 'roommate group.' Blankfein joined them, became more confident, developed a love of history and briefly became a lawyer before becoming a precious metals salesman at J. Aron & Co, a commodities broker bought by Goldman.
Despite presumably leaving the weed in the lonely dorm, Blankfein wasn't the finished article for a long time at Goldman Sachs. His book <https://www.amazon.co.uk/Streetwise-Lessons-Life-Goldman-Sachs-ebook/dp/B0F3MHP79J> includes a performance review from 2002, when he was aged 48, two years before he became Goldman president and COO. This review says that Blankfein was too "dominant in meetings," that he could be "overly harsh, adversarial, and intimidating," that he somehow needed to win every "debate or argument" and that he should become "more statesmanlike and positive."
Blankfein nonetheless became CEO of Goldman Sachs in 2006. This was fortunate because - by his own admission - "In a big crisis, I’m generally the most rational person around."
Blankfein wasn't raised upper middle class, or even middle class. He grew up in a cramped flat with furniture wrapped in plastic and a cockroach extermination business nearby. But Blankfein became CEO of Goldman. Now he has a flat in Miami where he spends the winter and a house in the Hamptons where he spends the summer. His daughter says he's "human." His wife has stayed with him for 42 years, even though, when a client called midway through Blankfein explaining to her that he had cancer in 2015, he "disconnected" and left her hanging. "What a terrible thing to do to her,” he laments.
Separately, no one knows how much Centerview settled ex-21-year-old banking analyst Kathryn Shiber's </news/centerview-analyst-sleep-working-hours>case for, but the suspicion is that it might have been for seven figures.
As we reported last week, Shiber </news/kathryn-shriber-centerview>spent less than three months at Centerview before being fired because she needed eight hours' sleep a night due to a preexisting anxiety disorder. She argued that she was discriminated against.
Centerview says it was confident of winning the case had it gone to trial, but that "we are nonetheless happy to put this distraction behind us and focus on delivering for our clients."
There are suggestions that banks won't be employing junior bankers with pre-existing conditions in the future. Shiber had wanted $5m+ in compensation. She's currently thought to be working for a dog food delivery company after a stint at Google.
Meanwhile...
If you work for Accenture and you don't adopt AI tools, you won't get promoted. (Financial Times) <https://www.ft.com/content/ac672f97-a603-4c56-afa3-4a5273d45674>
Private equity firms spent a record $348bn investing in software companies in 2021. “Have we had groupthink where you’ve got 30% or 40% of buyouts being in software? In retrospect, it was a pretty big red flag.” Apollo insists it has zero exposure. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-02-20/apollo-s-sambur-sees-prolonged-period-of-software-pain-for-private-equity>
UK bankers are paying marginal tax rates on their bonuses of 71% once student loans are added in. Moving to the Middle East is a "repeat conversation" as a result. (Financial Times) <https://www.ft.com/content/d3c861fa-ca18-415b-b98e-bfe464920c1a>
Retail banks want to build sales and trading businesses as revenues are steadier than before. At JPMorgan, the variation of quarterly trading revenues has fallen by a third over the past five years compared with the prior five — even as the revenue base has grown. (Financial Times) <https://www.ft.com/content/37889be3-2ace-4855-96aa-be34eb0bb80f>
Lloyd Blankfein is not a fan of loading pensions with private credit assets: "I think it’s crazy to put those assets there and I think it’s crazy from their point of view. They have nice lives, they make a fortune, their companies are huge, they already own their yachts and whatever it is they want...These securities are opaque and may be riskier than most, but to the extent you’re selling to institutions people don’t care that much. But if individuals lose money or insurance companies or real businesses lose money it’s terrible." (WSJ) <https://www.wsj.com/finance/investing/lloyd-blankfein-goldman-sachs-memoir-ceo-b36c0542?mod=hp_lead_pos8>
"I went fully “beige” and chose to do an accountancy degree because I believed that would win me a job, a career. I thought I was being sensible. Now I can't get a job." (The Times) <https://www.thetimes.com/life-style/work/article/lost-generation-jobs-mkprls8b5>
Even minimum wage jobs now require two hour interviews, online applications with 10 paragraphs on work experience and assessment days with 30 candidates solving anagrams and completing quizzes. (Guardian) <https://www.theguardian.com/money/2026/feb/18/entry-level-recruitment-low-paid-jobs-wage>

item.title: Hedge fund Brevan Howard lost a long-tenured macro portfolio manager
item.description:
Things have been looking up at hedge fund Brevan Howard, which generated returns of 4.4% in its Master Fund <https://www.ft.com/content/8fc6edbb-adb8-46a2-84f4-e2b0bbc50aa2>in January after a mere 0.8% gain in 2025. Despite this, a veteran portfolio manager is leaving.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Mike Graham, who has worked for Brevan Howard since joining as a graduate in 2007 has left Brevan Howard and is on a non-compete according to his LinkedIn profile.
Both Brevan Howard and Graham declined to comment.
Brevan Howard was founded in 2002. Graham wasn't there from the very beginning, but was there from the early days. Graham's entire career - save for a year in the British army - has been spent at Brevan Howard.
Brevan Howard has been making some changes this year. For example, Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-01-28/brevan-to-shut-golchin-s-hedge-fund-as-focus-turns-to-main-fund> reported in January that Fash Golchin, a star trader who was running an 'FG Fund' which included external capital, is now running the largest cash allocation from the $11.4bn Master Fund instead.

item.title: Wells Fargo halved US interns' hourly pay if they work more than 40 hour weeks
item.description:
Wells Fargo is growing its investment bank </news/what-is-an-investment-bank>, but it is being careful with pay for US interns.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Last year, Wells Fargo's investment bankers had their best year ever, as noted by the Wall Street Journal. <https://www.wsj.com/finance/banking/wells-fargo-mergers-aquisitions-deals-2025-8ce9d4fb?mod=series_bankmeltdown>The Financial Times <https://www.wsj.com/finance/banking/wells-fargo-mergers-aquisitions-deals-2025-8ce9d4fb?mod=series_bankmeltdown>says the bank has hired 125 managing directors since 2019 and that it plans to hire another 27 or so this year. And yet, Wells Fargo risks upsetting its investment banking interns. </news/internship-investment-bank>
Wells Fargo declined to comment for this article, but incoming US interns at its investment bank say they've been informed that they will receive a fixed rate of pay up to 40 hours a week, plus a supplementary hourly rate when they work beyond that. The supplementary rate will be 50% of regular pay.
For the standard 40 hour week, Wells Fargo's investment banking interns will receive $2.1k. This is equivalent to $52 an hour. But when they work more than 40 hours, they will receive at most $26 for each extra hour. The policy does not apply in California.
Goldman Sachs is understood to operate a similar policy. This has not been confirmed and it's not clear how long it's been in operation for.
The change means that interns who might have received an extra $1.6k a week for working 70 hours, will now only receive an extra $780. They are understandably unhappy as a result.
Most investment banks increased analyst salaries during the pandemic and hiked intern pay in proportion to this. Now it seems that banks are looking for methods of massaging pay back down again.

item.title: The Coffee Run: Why an investment banking analyst is going to war over eight hours of sleep
item.description:
We've launched a new weekly video series <https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLqJc_c5JT66B3Cp_26xsN8Xsv2EtvtEQQ> to accompany Morning Coffee, in which we look at one story in more depth to give you the full picture.
In finance, all eyes are on an impending court case involving boutique bank centerview partners. Former intern Kathryn Shiber is looking for $5m in damages over the way she was treated when joining the bank as an analyst.
Shiber was willing to work up to 98 hour weeks but that wasn't enough for Centerview. It's the way she wanted to work those hours that the bank disagreed with the most. Watch the video below to find out why.

item.title: The traders who will see their bonuses jump - and bankers who won't
item.description:
How was your bonus for 2025? You may know it already (fill out our bonus survey <https://www.smartsurvey.co.uk/s/DB8VZ6/>, if you have). Our Compensation Report will be out in a few weeks.
Click here to join the bubble by eFinancialCareers, our new anonymous community. <https://bubble.efinancialcareers.com/login?_gl=1*ut8n6u*_gcl_au*MTg4MDY2NTExNi4xNzYxNTU3NDIz*_ga*ODczNzI2NTA0LjE3NTM2OTk2ODI.*_ga_NZFQV4MXPM*czE3NjM2NDM0MDYkbzI1NiRnMSR0MTc2MzY0MzQzOSRqMjckbDAkaDMyMjc4OTUzMw..>✍️
Market Intelligence firm Tricumen suggest M&A bankers </news/what-are-ma-jobs> fared best in this year's bonus round, closely followed by commodities sales & trading </news/how-do-you-get-a-job-in-sales-and-trading-in-a-bank> professionals.
Tricumen estimated revenues per head by business area. As shown by the chart below, there were significant increases - 20% and 19% respectively - for M&A bankers and commodities traders, respectively. There were reductions in credit sales & trading revenue per person, as well as capital markets </news/capital-markets-jobs-ecm-and-dcm> revenue per person.
M&A bankers saw their average productivity increase by 20% - generating $1.2m on average per employee across 2025, compared to $1m per person in 2024. And 2026 will probably be even bigger - Goldman Sachs CEO David Solomon said <https://markets.chroniclejournal.com/chroniclejournal/article/marketminute-2026-2-16-the-dealmaking-renaissance-why-goldman-sachs-predicts-2026-will-be-a-record-breaking-year-for-m-and-a> at UBS Financial Services Conference earlier this month that 2026 would be a record year for M&A.
Commodities traders, who enjoyed the second biggest increases in revenue per person, could credit their increase to the volatility in the gold & silver market, according to Tricumen, although traders as a whole did well in 2025.
Equities also performed well as a category, with professionals in equity derivatives and prime services increasing their revenue per head by 11% and 13%, respectively.
Credit traders, on average, can expect a 4% decrease in their bonuses, according to Tricumen. Based on revenues per head, debt and equity capital markets bankers could expect their bonuses to fall by 1.6% and 1.9%, respectively.

item.title: HSBC fired more bankers before bonuses: "It's harsh and inhumane"
item.description:
HSBC is not the cosy place that it was. It is now a ruthless </news/hsbc-s-self-proclaimed-ruthless-ceo-is-not-its-most-feared-person> place run by hard-headed people prepared to pay zero bonuses </news/hsbc-s-zero-bonuses-risk-upsetting-already-traumatised-bankers>and make hard-headed decisions. Accordingly, having fired </news/hsbc-zero-bonuses-fired>many people on bonus day last year, HSBC is now cutting people just one week before it announces bonuses for 2025.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-02-19/hsbc-cuts-10-of-us-debt-capital-markets-team-amid-overhaul>reported the cuts yesterday. They afflict six people in the US debt capital markets </news/capital-markets-jobs-ecm-and-dcm> (DCM) team that has been the subject of 30+ exits in recent years. HSBC said: “We don’t comment on individuals. We’re committed to attracting and retaining talent as part of HSBC’s high-performance culture, and are proud of the progress of our DCM franchise.”
HSBC isn't discussing who's been let go, but we understand that the layoffs include Russell Ashcraft, a director in US debt syndicate who'd been at HSBC for 17 years; Matthew McNeilly, a DCM MD who'd been there for 19 years; and Rachel Solway, a director in Yankee debt capital markets who joined in 2022 and was promoted to director in 2024. Multiple insiders say Solway's displacement is unexpected: she was close to Patrice Altongy, the ex-Citi </news/hsbc-is-increasingly-run-by-people-who-ve-joined-from-citi>banker who's been running US DCM at HSBC since 2021.
McNeilly, Solway and Ashcraft didn't respond to a reques to comment. While they and others are leaving HSBC's US DCM business involuntarily, many tens of people have also left of their own accords. They include Jessica Gough </news/another-dcm-banker-resigned-from-hsbc-s-us-business>, who left last year and resurfaced at Scotiabank in Ireland. Although HSBC remains committed to the DCM business, there are suggestions that it's become a politicized place to work.
Insiders there say they're shocked that cuts are happening before bonuses once again. "There was an expectation that this was coming last year," says one. "But it seems very harsh to do this one week before announcing compensation. It's unusual to dump people one week before bonuses. Most banks are more humane."
HSBC isn't commenting on the reason for the cuts. The unspoken implication of referring to its "high performance culture" is that it's removing underperformers. It may also be the case that it's cutting expensive bankers with long tenure.

item.title: The ultimate post-bonus trade is leaving for a hedge fund in the UAE
item.description:
If you're a senior trader at an investment bank </news/what-is-an-investment-bank>in London and you've received your bonus </news/bonus-expectations-by-sector> for 2025, now is the time to secure an offer from a hedge fund </news/how-to-get-a-hedge-fund-job> in Dubai </news/hedge-funds-dubai-abu-dhabi>or Abu Dhabi. It's not just that you won't pay tax </news/hedge-fund-managers-leave-london-tax>on future earnings while you're working there; you can also get an upfront payment that compensates for bonuses left on the table at previous employers, tax free.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
London traders making the move say the tax free buyout is the main incentive for going to the UAE at all. "When you move to Dubai during your garden leave period and the fund you join pays you an upfront bonus and buys out your previous pay, it is subject to taxes in your new resident country," says one senior hedge fund manager.
Income taxes in the UAE are 0%. As long as you have tax residency in the country - which typically means staying there for a year, you benefit from this on your buyout.
"If you leave a bank and you have $2m in deferred, you can get it bought out tax free and have the vesting accelerated in Dubai," explains an ex-bank trader at a hedge fund in London. "It's why everyone wants to go there," he says.
The same applies when you leave a hedge fund in London. If you're bought out for $20m </news/hedge-fund-bonus> by a hedge fund in Dubai or Abu Dhabi, it's all tax free. If you move to a fund in London, you'll pay $9m in tax.
The senior hedge fund manager says this makes moving to Dubai a no-brainer. "This is why the UK government is talking about imposing an exit tax," he says.
The UAE's popularity with portfolio managers seeking to cash in is also why funds are increasingly obliged to open UAE offices. Citadel, for example, spent a long time without a Dubai office, but is finally opening in the UAE later this year.

item.title: Banking spring weeks: What you need to know
item.description:
If you’re a first-year undergraduate student at university interested in getting into investment banking </news/the-investment-banking-recruitment-process>, you might be mentally preparing to start your spring week </news/finance/investment-bank-spring-week>.
Click here to join the bubble by eFinancialCareers, our new anonymous community. <https://bubble.efinancialcareers.com/login?_gl=1*ut8n6u*_gcl_au*MTg4MDY2NTExNi4xNzYxNTU3NDIz*_ga*ODczNzI2NTA0LjE3NTM2OTk2ODI.*_ga_NZFQV4MXPM*czE3NjM2NDM0MDYkbzI1NiRnMSR0MTc2MzY0MzQzOSRqMjckbDAkaDMyMjc4OTUzMw..>✍️
If you’re also in your first year at university and you don't even know what a spring week is, bad luck.
Spring weeks, known as sophomore internships in the USA, are an integral, and increasingly important, part of the investment banking </news/the-investment-banking-recruitment-process> recruitment cycle. Many banks rely on them to recruit summer interns </news/internship-investment-bank>, and therefore – by proxy – junior bankers </news/i-worked-110-hours-a-week-as-a-junior-banker-in-london-and-then-quit>.
Therefore, if you don't have a banking spring week, it's harder to get a banking internship. And if you don't have a banking internship, it's harder to get a banking job when you graduate. So – if it’s not too late for you – what do you need to know about spring weeks?
What is a spring week in a bank?
Spring weeks are short internships, usually lasting just a few days or a week, set around springtime. They are an opportunity for a firm to get to know you, and potentially accelerate you through the summer internship recruitment process the following year. If you do well in a spring week, banks may even offer you a place on a summer internship outright, although only a minority of students benefit from this.
One student, who completed a spring internship at a major bank in London, told us that his spring week was “an informational session,” and was more focused on the bank advertising to you – there were “different panels discussing the bank’s culture, divisions’ roles."
Another student, with a number of different spring weeks under her belt, agreed. “I spent most of my time listening to panel talks introducing the company’s business lines,” she said. “We also did job simulations and networking sessions to construct early understanding of the industry.”
Networking is a big part of the spring week. You’ll meet people more senior to you. The students we spoke to for this article also all said that they were in touch with some of their fellow interns, forming a part of their professional network going forward.
How do you apply for a spring week in a bank?
You need to apply for spring weeks as soon as they open, which is usually in October the year before the week takes place. Closing dates for applications vary but are often November. That means, for a spring week in 2027, you’ll have to apply in October 2026.
Spring week applications generally involve three stages: an online assessment, HireVue </news/2022/10/hirevue-interview-banking> interview, and a human interview </news/70-top-banking-interview-questions-and-how-to-answer-them>, either digitally or in-person.
There isn't a specific profile of the ideal spring week candidate, and you don't need to study a specific subject to get offered the opportunity. "The recruiters there explicitly said that they were looking for high potential candidates, with great academics, but who perhaps hadn’t considered consulting as a career choice," one student who did a spring week (at a major consulting firm) told us. "As a result, they really didn’t have many technical expectations coming into the spring week."
How many spring weeks can you do?
Hypothetically – and ideally – as many as possible.
One student that we spoke to did multiple spring internships – more than five – across multiple industries, including investment banking. “I wanted to try out different industries to test which ones are my favorites,” he said. “University students like me are usually open to various roles, but at the same time I want to find the most suitable types for me as soon as possible, so that I can target my job hunting at those specific areas.”
No matter the exact number of spring week internships you do apply to, you should be applying to as many as possible. Last year, a poll of students in a private group chat showed that around a quarter didn’t receive a single spring internship offer, and less than 45% received a single internship offer. Only around 30% received an offer for multiple internships.
How can a spring week help you?
Spring weeks are very useful. As we mentioned before, it can lead to exposure to varied industries, the chance to network with your peers and (future) colleagues and maybe learn a few things in the process.
Most importantly, a spring week can lead to a summer internship. This varies quite wildly between banks. Some banks, such as Morgan Stanley, recruit their summer intern cohort exclusively from their spring intern class. Some don’t recruiter summer interns from spring weeks at all.
Many banks fast-track all their spring interns into the summer internship recruitment process – for example, automatically offering them assessment centre </news/investment-bank-assessment-centre> interviews, allowing them to skip much of the application process. They also offer a small number summer internships directly, but the exact number varies significantly between banks, and not all do this.
How should I prepare for a spring week?
You don’t need a strong technical foundation for a spring week. Banks know you are a first-year student, and they’re more interested in who you are as an individual.
“The bank didn’t have any expectations of prior technical or advanced markets knowledge,” one spring intern told us. “They never ask advanced technicals at spring conversions or summer internship interviews.”
Another student concurred. You need “an interest in the industry and some very basic understandings, like the recent market news, if you’re on the markets track.” On top of that, “behaviorally, you will need to collaborate well in teams, handle conflict with your colleagues, follow company rules, deliver hard work, and attention to detail.”
What if you don’t covert your spring week into a summer internship?
If you don’t get an offer at the end of the internship, or if the spring week you completed didn’t directly recruit for a summer program, don’t be discouraged.
A report last year </news/internship-applications-banks> showed that just one quarter of accepted applicants to a financial services summer internship had completed a spring week previously. Those applicants that did have a spring internship, however, were nearly twice as likely to receive an interview </news/internship-applications-banks> to a summer program. A spring internship is useful, but not critical, to getting a financial services job.
What do you do if you don’t get a spring week?
If you don’t manage to get a spring week, it’s not the end of the world. For one, you should definitely be looking for finance internships, both spring and summer, no matter what, even if they’re in fintech or at firms you’re not particularly interested in.
Another option is doing an off-cycle internship </news/no-spring-internship-bank>. These are arguably more valuable from a learning point of view than the traditional spring/summer internship cycle. If you’re EMEA-based, doing them outside of London might even lead to unique networking opportunities, given the relative sizes of teams and how much closer you are on the chain of command to the decision makers and revenue generators of the firm.

item.title: Morning Coffee: Can 21-year-old bankers on $175k complain about 120 hour weeks? The riches of Steve Cohen
item.description:
As we reported yesterday </news/kathryn-shriber-centerview>, the court case concerning Kathryn Shiber, a former 21-year-old investment banking </news/what-is-an-investment-bank> analyst </news/finance/banks-weird-hierarchies-analysts-associates-vps-mds-really> at boutique bank </news/boutique-investment-banks>Centerview, turns up in a Manhattan federal court next week. Shiber says her three month stint at Centerview involved "discriminatory animus" and ruined her career and her mental health. She wants $5m in damages.
Get Morning Coffee </myefc/preferences/subscribe?newsletter=network_newsletter>☕ in your inbox. Sign up here. </myefc/preferences/subscribe?newsletter=network_newsletter>
The court will be heard by a jury and much will depend upon the jury's sympathies, or not, with investment banking culture. As Business Insider <https://www.businessinsider.com/wall-street-work-culture-shiber-centerview-trial-lawsuit-2026-2> notes, both Shiber and Centerview are touchy about who sits on the jury: Shiber seems to want people sympathetic to discrimination law; Centerview suggests asking whether people on the jury have ever asked for an accommodation at work due to a disability.
Shiber says she was disabled when she joined Centerview. A diagnosed anxiety disorder meant she needed eight hours sleep each night. Centerview tried to accommodate her, and then decided it couldn't. It abruptly fired her by Zoom, telling her that she couldn't do the job and should never have accepted it.
Much will depend, therefore, upon sympathy with Shiber's situation. And that will depend upon whether jurors judge that a 21-year-old likely earning over $175k a year while formatting PowerPoints, should have taken a job that makes getting a steady eight hours sleep a night a challenge.
We don't know exactly how much Shiber was paid. However, Centerview is known as one of the most generous boutiques to work for. In 2023, Instagram account Litquidity <https://www.instagram.com/litquidity/> said </news/2023/03/highest-paying-investment-bank> analysts at Centerview were earning an average of $205k in salaries plus bonuses </news/banker-bonuses>, and that third year associates </news/banks-weird-hierarchies-analysts-associates-vps-mds> there were on $800k. The tacit expectation is that young people will work unusually long hours in return for such riches. Business Insider <https://www.businessinsider.com/wall-street-work-culture-shiber-centerview-trial-lawsuit-2026-2> notes that Centerview's court depositions state that 60-120 hours are the norm, that 24 hour days are possible but rare, that hours can be erratic, and that junior bankers must check-in before signing off.
In Shiber's defence, she says it wasn't the hours that were the problem. It was the sleep. She was willing to work from 9am to midnight seven days a week, so long as she slept eight hours a night. This implies 98 hour weeks, and only one hour a day outside of work. This wasn't enough for Centerview. Shiber claim <https://storage.courtlistener.com/recap/gov.uscourts.nysd.559015/gov.uscourts.nysd.559015.1.0_2.pdf>s that Centerview told her the job would, “require many 120-hour weeks over the 3 years which you should have known”
Shiber really wanted to be an analyst at Centerview and said it was her "dream." She received the job offer while she was a student at Dartmouth in quantitative social science and studio art and completed 50 hours of courses and six exams before joining. She says she was keen. She just didn't want to destroy her health with lack of sleep. Her case will help determine whether grinding on through is part of the junior banker deal.
Separately Stevie-Steve Cohen made $9m a day last year, which was more than the $8.2m a day made by Izzy Englander at Millennium or the $6.4m a day made by Ken Griffin at Citadel. </news/ken-griffin-s-message-to-young-people-coming-age-in-a-complicated-world>
Cohen himself once worked 90 hour weeks </news/steve-cohen-point72> and was predicted to die at his desk, but he has taken a step back in recent years and is now into gentle mentoring instead of relentless trading. His high earnings were not the result of owning New York Mets, but of owning his multistrategy hedge fund </news/big-multistrategy-hedge-funds>, Point72. Bloomberg notes that Point72, which manages $45.7bn in assets, has 3,000 staff, 190 trading pods, and a quantitative business - Cubist - which employs 600 people.
With his money, Cohen could buy $1m hotdogs at a baseball game every day for year.
Meanwhile...
Damilare Ajao </news/is-it-worth-working-for-goldman-sachs>worked at Commerzbank and said a female employee grabbed his crotch. She didn't he was sentenced to 20 months in prison for making false allegations. That was cut to eight months even though he has “shown no remorse or understanding of what he has done is wrong.”(Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-02-19/ex-commerzbank-analyst-s-jail-term-over-bogus-claims-reduced>
Shares in Blue Owl, Apollo, Blackstone, Ares and KKR all plunged yesterday. It was Blue Owl's fault: it restricted redemptions from a retail fund. Blue Owl's shares are down 15% so far this month. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-02-19/asset-manager-shares-plunge-as-blue-owl-curbs-fund-redemptions>
Blue Owl will permanently restrict retail investors from withdrawing cash from its inaugural private retail debt fund. It had said they could withdraw at quarterly interval, now it's promising episodic payments as it sells down assets in coming quarters and years. (Financial Times) <https://www.ft.com/content/b2f299f6-2a82-4a43-bcbf-86cac3937550>
It's a fine time to be a macro hedge fund manager. Asfandyar Nadeem, ex-Brevan Howard PM and founder of Deem Global says: “The current environment is so rich. It is global, differentiated, and deeply structural. And it rewards those willing to think seriously about governments — not as background noise, but as the primary drivers of markets once again.” (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-02-19/nadeem-s-macro-hedge-fund-deem-closing-to-new-cash-at-3-billion?utm_medium=email&utm_source=author_alert&utm_term=260219&utm_campaign=author_19458291>
BNP Paribas now has 21 people in Miami working with hedge funds and it wants to add more. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-02-19/bnp-plans-to-expand-miami-office-following-47-revenue-growth?srnd=phx-industries-finance>

item.title: Where is the private equity partner excited about the deal that lost $1bn?
item.description:
What happens when you're a partner at a private equity firm and a deal that you worked on makes a 10 figure loss? In theory, private equity dealmakers have their incentives aligned via carried interest. </news/private-equity-carried-interest> In fact, they may not hang around long enough to see the loss on a particular deal crystallize.
Get Morning Coffee </myefc/preferences/subscribe?newsletter=network_newsletter>☕ in your inbox. Sign up here. </myefc/preferences/subscribe?newsletter=network_newsletter>
Timothy Franks had a long and lucrative career in private equity. Franks, who did not respond to an invitation to comment for this article, began his career in 1999 after a brief time in investment banking </news/what-is-an-investment-bank> at Deutsche Bank and then Credit Suisse. He became a partner at Advent International and then at KKR.
One of the investments Franks was involved in at KKR lost $1.3bn this week, but he may not mind. He left the fund a year after it was made.
As head of the EMEA consumer group at KKR, Franks fronted the fund's €1.1bn ($1.3bn) investment in Dutch e-bike maker Accell Group in 2022, and lists it among his achievements on his LinkedIn profile. When the deal was done, Franks said he was excited for its potential. “ Accell Group’s transport and mobility solutions have been a thematic investment focus for KKR for some time, and we believe that the bicycle sector and e-bikes in particular will play an increasingly important role in dealing with some of the major challenges the world is facing today...", he declared, adding that KKR's investment would help Accell to realize "its full potential as a global industry leader and sustainable innovator.”
Accell did not achieve its full potential. After over-ordering inventory, it instead faced a sales slump that threatened it with bankruptcy <https://www.ft.com/content/acd62c0a-7c99-4864-9d77-f94b66664bda> until its debts were restructured this week, resulting in KKR losing its $1.3bn investment.
The episode is a reminder that private equity is not a one way street. Accell's issues started surfacing in mid-2023; Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2023-07-12/kkr-s-franks-departs-as-head-of-uk-and-ireland-private-equity> reported that Franks decided to leave in July of the same year. His exit may have been coincidental. An entire team at KKR worked on the deal.
Some of Franks' other investments at KKR have fared much better. He also participated in KKR's purchase of a 60% stake in Wella in 2020. Reuters <https://www.reuters.com/business/finance/kkr-prepares-opi-owner-wella-company-us-ipo-sources-say-2026-02-03/> reported in January that Wella is preparing for an IPO which will value the company at far more than KKR paid for it.
After leaving KKR, Franks became CEO of "purpose driven" investment firm Jacobs Holdings, but he stepped down from that role last year.
Have a confidential story, tip, or comment you’d like to share? Contact: +44 7537 182250 (SMS, Whatsapp or voicemail). Telegram: @SarahButcher. Signal: sarahbutcher.22 Click here to fill in our anonymous form </about/editor-tips>, or email editortips@efinancialcareers.com.
Bear with us if you leave a comment at the bottom of this article: comments are moderated intermittently by human beings. Reuters <https://www.reuters.com/business/finance/kkr-prepares-opi-owner-wella-company-us-ipo-sources-say-2026-02-03/> reported in January that Wella is preparing for an IPO which will value the company at far more than KKR paid for it.
After leaving KKR, Franks became CEO of "purpose driven" investment firm Jacobs Holdings, but he stepped down from that role last year.

item.title: How to write a resume for a job in quant finance
item.description:
Getting a quant </news/quant-jobs-finance> job at a bank, hedge fund </news/all-the-jobs-in-hedge-funds> or trading firm </news/high-frequency-trading-hiring-and-pay> is an arduous process, but it'll never start if your CV </news/2022/06/banking-resume-guide> never makes it through the screening process. In such a complex and opaque industry, how do you write a resume that stands out? We've spoken to recruiters and industry insiders to find out.
Follow eFinancialCareers on Google News here. <https://news.google.com/publications/CAAqMggKIixDQklTR3dnTWFoY0tGV1ZtYVc1aGJtTnBZV3hqWVhKbFpYSnpMbU52YlNnQVAB?ceid=GB:en&oc=3>
One New York headhunter, speaking anonymously, told us that the "format of the resume </news/finance/50-ways-to-improve-your-resume-to-land-a-financial-services-job> is completely irrelevant, it just needs to be clean </news/goldman-sachs-cv-template>and show quality throughout their education and work history." James Holland, director of London-based recruitment firm Quant Capital agreed. When you're writing a quant CV, Holland said: "most companies want to see clearly where you have worked and what you've been doing," he said. "This shouldn't take more than a page."
When it comes to education </news/the-20-universities-jane-street-loves-most-for-its-quants-and-engineers>, Holland said firms are paying attention to "where you've been educated, what grade you got and when you went there." How detailed you need to be varies on the role and firm you're applying for. Jane Street </news/how-to-get-a-job-at-jane-street> employees, for example, have previously suggested that the modules you choose are more important than the degree itself; you'd be wise to include them when applying to a junior role at a trading firm, but perhaps less so for a senior quantitative portfolio manager job at a hedge fund.
When it comes to technology, Holland said you should avoid listing "a million things you've only played with. Be honest about your real strengths." Don't list Python </news/programming-languages-for-a-career-in-finance> unless you can "write hardcore production Python code." An easy way to do this is by listing technologies on a role-by-role basis; Kirsty Tutton, a director at Selby Jennings, previously told us </news/2023/01/best-banking-cv-engineering> "banking clients like to see the tech stack used for each role as opposed to a big list"
Holland said "people are also looking at GitHubs </news/hedge-fund-tech-cv-interview-advice#:~:text=Going%20into%20the,your%20own%20risk.> more and more." It's becoming a necessity to include one outside of pure alpha generation </news/what-is-a-quant-researcher-hedge-fund> roles. Working on open-source projects is highly valued, but you should also include personal projects if you have any. "It gives a few more more strings to your bow and shows you're interested in what you do," he said.
As with all CVs, it's important to quantify your achievements wherever possible. This can be hard when you've worked or interned at a highly secretive firm. Speaking via an AMA in The Bubble, our new anonymous community <https://bubble.efinancialcareers.com/t/ama-im-a-quant-career-coach-supporting-internship-job-search-mfe-admissions/5111/11>, quant careers coach Ash Cross gave some workarounds. You can estimate the number of hours of work saved by technology you've built, talk about how widely your tools have been adopted across your business or provide percentage improvements in the accuracy and stability of your models. You should ask your former manager if you're unsure, but Cross says you should talk "about the general process and the impact you made, not the frameworks used."
Don't worry if your resume looks too vague. Holland said firms "understand the game; we're not expecting a CV that details everything and gives away the secrets." If you're being asked to disclose them in your resume or during an interview, it's a bit of a red flag.
'Interests' sections on quant CVs are a contentious topic, but can go a long way in humanizing you. Holland said that it's important that your interests are "recent, relevant and something you can talk about." For example, competitive chess </news/2022/12/how-to-get-a-trading-job-at-citadel-securities> is very popular among quants and "it's a good thing to put on there if you can genuinely talk about it... you never know who you'll meet in an interview." Interests without direct bearing on quant finance should only be mentioned if they require a high level of expertise (e.g. restoring classic cars) or if you perform at a particularly high level (e.g. professional/amateur powerlifting, as opposed to a 'Passion for fitness').
Have a confidential story, tip, or comment you’d like to share? item.title: Centerview Partners' emotionally distressed analyst wants her therapist to testify
item.description:
Kathryn Shiber </news/open-ai-junior-bankers> only worked for boutique </news/boutique-investment-banks> investment bank </news/what-is-an-investment-bank> Centerview Partners for three months before she was fired, but in a New York court case whch the Financial Times <https://www.ft.com/content/60b3dd1c-112a-470c-aa77-1bc697d13cf7>says is due to start next week, Shiber is seeking $5m in damages for - among other things - all the money she might have earned during a 10 year career there. Whether or not she succeeds may depend in part upon her therapist.
Get Morning Coffee </myefc/preferences/subscribe?newsletter=network_newsletter>☕ in your inbox. Sign up here. </myefc/preferences/subscribe?newsletter=network_newsletter>
Shiber says her short time as an investment banking analyst formatting PowerPoint slides at Centerview left her with issues. Her original filing in 2021 <https://storage.courtlistener.com/recap/gov.uscourts.nysd.559015/gov.uscourts.nysd.559015.1.0_2.pdf> cites,"damages to the quality of her life, her self-esteem, self-respect and financial and emotional well-being. She says she was subjected to "humiliating and demeaning" conduct at Centervew, which will "remain a source of humiliation, distress and financial loss," for the future.
Shiber - who was already suffering from a diagnosed mood and anxiety disorder when she joined the firm aged 21, is asking her ex-therapist, Kylee Mueggenburg, to testify to the emotional distress she suffered as a result of her brief time at the boutique. Centerview, in turn, is arguing that Mueggenburg's testimony should be excluded from the trial because it says Shiber disclosed Mueggenburg's identity too late in the process. Centerview also says that Mueggenburg "blindly relied on representations made by Ms. Shiber....and did not consider alternative sources of Ms. Shiber’s alleged distress." Mueggenburg allegedly didn't even know that Shiber had lost her job, for example.
Mueggenburg - who is based in San Fransciso - describes herself <https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/therapists/kylee-mueggenburg-san-francisco-ca/1557829>as helping young adults experiencing anxiety and life transitions and passionate about helping people navigate trauma. After being fired by Centerview, Shiber worked in the finance group at Google. The FT notes that she's earned $582k in the five years since leaving the boutique.
Shiber's case hinges around whether Centerview should have made more effort to accommodate her need to sleep for at least eight hours a night to stop her anxiety from spiralling.
Shiber claims that once she started working at Centerview it became quickly apparent that "employees were expected, at times, to work 24 hours in a day or across multiple days without rest or stopping." She says that she herself worked from 8am to 1am for "two or three days in a row" during one week and that after logging off at 1am when she thought all her work was finally done, her associate reprimanded her for not informing him first. Shiber then went to HR, who arranged for her to have a "hard stop" between midnight and 9am. She proceeded to work "from 9:00 am to midnight each day, seven days a week," including a whole Labor Day weekend. She was fired around two weeks later.
If Shiber's case succeeds, other junior bankers </news/finance/banks-weird-hierarchies-analysts-associates-vps-mds-really> with anxiety will also be able to demand eight hours' sleep. Centerview is arguing that it doesn't owe her many millions of dollars because she only worked there briefly and "identified no evidence indicating she would have completed the three-year analyst program, let alone received a promotion to associate."
The FT notes that Centerview has also raised questions about the amount of time Shiber spent on Instagram and Tiktok.

item.title: Singapore’s home hero hedge fund picks up a Senior PM from Millennium
item.description:
Dymon Asia Capital, the Singapore-based hedge fund </news/how-to-get-a-hedge-fund-job> growing all over Asia </news/singapore-hedge-fund-hiring-dymon>, had a great 2025, with its multi-strategy fund returning 18% across the year. It grew across the year – and it seems to be growing into 2026, too.
Click here to join the bubble by eFinancialCareers, our new anonymous community. <https://bubble.efinancialcareers.com/login?_gl=1*ut8n6u*_gcl_au*MTg4MDY2NTExNi4xNzYxNTU3NDIz*_ga*ODczNzI2NTA0LjE3NTM2OTk2ODI.*_ga_NZFQV4MXPM*czE3NjM2NDM0MDYkbzI1NiRnMSR0MTc2MzY0MzQzOSRqMjckbDAkaDMyMjc4OTUzMw..>✍️
Wissam Mezrani joined Dymon Asia Capital last month in Singapore as a portfolio manager, specialising in cross-asset derivative strategies. He joined the firm from Millennium, where he spent just under two years as a senior portfolio manager. Dymon does not use the "senior" PM title, although it does also operate pods, like many other multi-strategy funds </news/biggest-multistrategy-hedge-funds>.
Before joining Millennium, Mezrani spent four years at Citi in Hong Kong and then Singapore. In both markets, he was the firm’s head of quantitative market making – presumably locally. He also had stints as a trader at SocGen and Credit Suisse.
At Dymon, Mezrani will reunite with Anass Hamedi </news/dymon-capital-asia>, a fellow graduate of grande école </news/2023/02/best-grande-ecole-finance> CentraleSupélec. They worked together closely at Millennium – Hamedi was part of Mezrani’s pod – and they are working together at Dymon, too.
Dymon’s appetite for growth seems insatiable. The fund hired at least 19 portfolio managers </news/dymon-capital-asia> in 2025, as part of a 100-ish growth in headcount. It seems to be on the same path in 2026 – and with its multi-strategy fund posting a record 5% return in January 2026, things seem to be on track.

item.title: Eisler Capital's ill-fated head of HR says it was a "formative" experience
item.description:
When Joanna Hay arrived at Eisler Capital as head of HR in September 2025, she possibly thought she was joining a small but thriving hedge fund </news/finance/how-to-get-a-hedge-fund-job>that needed to hire a few more people after its key portfolio managers ran away earlier in the year. </news/as-portfolio-managers-leave-eisler-capital-contemplation-of-bonus-clawbacks>
Get Morning Coffee </myefc/preferences/subscribe?newsletter=network_newsletter>☕ in your inbox. Sign up here. </myefc/preferences/subscribe?newsletter=network_newsletter>
Instead, Hay says she found herself "supporting the fund through its eventual unwinding." Writing on LinkedIn, Hay says this was "difficult and challenging but turned out to be one of the most positive formative experiences of my career."
Eisler Capital announced its intention to close </news/eisler-capital-hedge-fund-closing> at the end of September 2025, so Hay presumably had no more than 30 days there before the reality of her role became apparent. She says she and her team approached the process of parting company with Eisler's staff with "rigor and efficiency," and "sustained the high level of operation Eisler Capital was known for."
We haven't spoken to Eisler Capital for this article, but many of its people have since turned up elsewhere. Valdo Durrleman, </news/valdo-durrleman-brevan-howard> its second most important strat </news/eisler-capital>, has for example, recently resurfaced at Brevan Howard.
Eisler Capital has sadly become the poster child for multistrategy hedge funds </news/big-multistrategy-hedge-funds>gone wrong. Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-01-07/eisler-lost-14-3-last-year-as-hedge-fund-firm-closed-down> reported that the fund lost 14.3% in its final year. Things deteriorated as the expense of paying portfolio managers on the pass-through model </news/hedge-fund-pass-throughs>weighed on insipid returns after all the most profitable portfolio managers escaped </news/lewis-morton-eisler>. Star performer Lewis Morton is now at Taula Capital with "humble" founder Diego Megia </news/taula-capital-hedge-fund> instead. Most people left before the end of December </news/eisler-capital-is-officially-cutting-79-people-in-new-york> and Eisler's fancy London office </news/eisler-capital-london-office>is now occupied by rival hedge fund Verition, which previously occupied the floor below and could be observed through a glass portal. To the extent that anyone is still working on the wind down, we understand that they are at home.
Meanwhile, Hay herself has got a new job. She is now head of HR at Helios Investment Partners, an African private investment firm. Other funds thinking of winding down at speed may want to bear her expertise in mind.

item.title: JPMorgan trader suffered "dehumanising travel experiences" as a result of her disability
item.description:
One of the benefits of working at a bank like JPMorgan </news/jpmorgan-pub>is that it's a global institution, and you have the opportunity to move to a new city for work. When you suffer from a disability </news/2022/08/work-in-ma-chronically-tired>, this benefit comes with a lot of costs; Julie Andrieu, an exotics trader </news/how-do-you-get-a-job-in-sales-and-trading-in-a-bank> at JPMorgan, said she experiences "dehumanising travel experiences several times a year" as a result of poor accomodation for her spinal muscular atrophy.
Follow eFinancialCareers on Google News here. <https://news.google.com/publications/CAAqMggKIixDQklTR3dnTWFoY0tGV1ZtYVc1aGJtTnBZV3hqWVhKbFpYSnpMbU52YlNnQVAB?ceid=GB:en&oc=3>
Andrieu works in JPMorgan's London office, having moved there from France after completing a masters in financial engineering (MFE) </news/top-masters-in-financial-engineering-courses> in 2022. She "travel[s] regularly between London and the South of France to see [her] family" but said that she has had to raise formal complaints with Heathrow Airport multiple times over her treatment. This week, she said she took a British Airways flight back to London and had to wait in the plane for an hour "because no ramp had been organised to retrieve my wheelchair from the hold, despite the cabin crew proactively alerting ground staff in advance." When she got off the plane, the assistance staff "were so poorly trained that they nearly dropped me."
Andrieu said she now dreads flying, "not because of turbulence, but because of systems that consistently fail disabled passengers." This isn't just problematic for her personal life; Andrieu said that disabled people "want to travel for business. We want to lead companies. We want to contribute to economic growth." A lack of accommodation for their needs obstructs this. British Airways did not respond to a request for comment.
In 2024, Andrieu was named a disability champion by JPMorgan and praised for her efforts to help hire disabled students. The bank has been quietly distancing itself from DEI practices in recent years, but this has usually been in relation to gender or ethnic diversity criteria </news/diversity-hiring-banks>. The bank's decision to bring staff into the office 5 days per week </news/jpmorgan-remote-work-banking> has been criticised for its lack of inclusiveness for disabled staff, but in Jamie Dimon's famous rant </news/jamie-dimon-work-from-home> against hybrid work, he did admit that the bank had learned "flexibility" over covid. "Particularly for, it’s usually women, caregivers, we understand there will be flexibility," he said.
